PT 365
 - Polity, Governance and Social Issues
1. FUNCTIONING OF PARLIAMENT, STATE
LEGISLATURE AND LOCAL GOVERNMENT
1.1. 50 YEARS OF NATIONAL EMERGENCY
Why in the News?
2025 marked 50 years since the
imposition of the National
Emergency, declared on June 25,
1975, and lasting until March 21, 1977.
About Emergency in India
• Source: Suspension of
Fundamental Rights (FRs) during
Emergency borrowed from
Weimar Constitution of
Germany.
• Constitutional Basis: Articles
352–360 under Part XVIII of
Constitution.
• Instances: National Emergency proclaimed three times—1962 (Chinese Aggression), 1971 (Pakistan
war), and 1975 (internal emergency).
Aspect Proclamation of National Emergency
Authority Proclaimed by the President after receiving written decision of the Union
Cabinet.
Parliamentary
Approval
Must be approved by both Houses within 1 month.
Duration Valid for 6 months; can be extended indefinitely with parliamentary approval every
6 months (44th Constitutional Amendment, 1978).
When Lok Sabha is
dissolved
Continues until 30 days after first sitting of reconstituted Lok Sabha, provided
Rajya Sabha has approved it.
Majority Required Special Majority: majority of total membership + 2/3rd of members present and
voting.
Revocation Can be revoked by President at any time without parliamentary approval; Lok
Sabha may disapprove continuation by simple majority.
Centre–State
Relations
Centre may issue executive directions on any subject; Parliament can legislate
on State List (Article 250); President may modify revenue distribution.
Tenure of
Legislatures
Lok Sabha & State Legislative Assemblies may be extended one year at a time, but
not beyond 6 months after Emergency ends.
Fundamental Rights Enforcement of FRs may be suspended under Article 359, except Articles 20 & 21.

5 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS

 - Polity, Governance and Social Issues
Other Changes brought through 44th Amendment Act (1978):
• For Emergency: Union Cabinet to communicate to President in writing.
o Mandatory Parliamentary approval within a month by special majority for Emergency
Proclamations.
o Restricted scope of Article 359 i.e. Article 20 and Article 21 remain enforceable during emergency.
o Article 358 automatically suspended rights under Article 19 (only for Emergency imposed due to
war or external aggression).
• Omitted right to property as FR and made it Constitutional right under Article 300A.
• Removal of Article 257A: Allowed Government of India to deploy any armed force of Union or any other
force to deal with any grave situation of law and order in any State.
• Term of Lok Sabha: Back to 5 years from 6 years by amending Articles 83 and 172.
• Restored judicial review: To review election disputes concerning President and Vice-President.
1.2. ASSENT TO STATE BILLS
Why in the News?
Supreme Court in 16th Presidential Reference (under Article 143) held that Court cannot impose any timelines
for decisions of President and Governor on granting assent to Bills under Articles 200/201 of Constitution.
Key Highlights of Judgment on 16th Presidential Reference
• ‘Deemed Assent’ Unconstitutional: Courts cannot assume assent to Bill merely because court-ordered
time lapsed, as it infringes constitutional powers of President/Governor, violates separation of powers and
federal constitutional scheme.
• Governors/President Cannot Sit on Bills Indefinitely: As it violates federalism, legislature’s will and
constitutional morality.
• Limited Judicial Review: Courts can intervene if Governor/President acts with malafide intent and review is
confined to procedure, not merits.
• Supreme Advice not required: President need not seek Supreme Court’s advice whenever Governor
reserves a Bill for his/her assent as subjective satisfaction of President is sufficient.

Note: Constitution of India (under Article 163 (2)) explicitly mentions that in certain spheres the Governor of a
State acts in his/her own discretion. However, Constitution does not empower President to independently
(suo motu) take up a State bill unless it has first been reserved and forwarded by the Governor.
Other Key Highlights of State of Tamil Nadu vs Governor of Tamil Nadu Judgement
Aspect Detail
Inaction is
unconstitutional
Governor/President cannot exercise Pocket or Absolute Veto on Bills under Articles
200 & 201; inaction can be challenged via writ of mandamus.
Note: Writ of mandamus cannot be issued:
• against private individual or body.
• to enforce departmental instruction that does not possess statutory force.
• when duty is discretionary to enforce contractual obligation.
• against President of India or State Governors.

7 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS
PT 365
 - Polity, Governance and Social Issues
• - against Chief Justice of a High Court acting in a judicial capacity.
Governor can’t
reserve re-enacted
Bill
Governor may reserve a Bill only once; after re-enactment, it cannot be reserved
again unless materially altered.
No absolute
discretion under
Article 200
Governor is bound by Council of Ministers’ advice except in constitutionally specified
cases:
• HC powers,
• Articles 288 (taxes on water or electricity),
• Article 360 (Financial Emergency),
• Article 254(2) (Concurrent List),
• Article 304(b) (Interstate trade and commerce),
• Articles 31A/31C
• Unconstitutional Bills.
Discretion subject
to judicial review
While assent itself isn’t justiciable, withholding or reserving assent can be challenged
in courts.
President &
Supreme Court
consultation (Art.
143)
On Bills reserved for unconstitutionality, President should seek SC’s advisory
opinion on constitutionality; opinion has strong persuasive value.
Note: Under Article 361 (1), Governor of a State is not answerable to any court for exercise and performance
of powers and duties of his/her office. However, actions of government remain open to judicial review. Article
361(2) provides absolute immunity to the Governor from criminal proceedings while in office.
1.3. OFFICE OF VICE PRESIDENT
Why in the news?
Recently, C.P. Radhakrishnan elected as the 15th Vice-President of India.
Election Procedure of Vice President:
• Nomination: Requires 20 proposers and 20 seconders; ₹15,000 deposit is mandatory.
• Voting: Conducted in Parliament House using special ECI-supplied pens. First preference vote is
compulsory.
• Supervision: ECI appoints the Returning Officer (Secretary General, Rajya Sabha) and observers.
• Key Safeguards followed in Elections:
o Provisions of the Anti-Defection Law are not applicable.
o Violation of secrecy or use of unauthorized pen invalidates the vote.
o Bribery or undue influence is grounds for election challenge under Presidential and Vice-Presidential
Elections Act, 1952.
Note: 39th Amendment to Constitution removed the authority of Supreme Court to adjudicate petitions
regarding elections of President, Vice-President, Prime Minister and Speaker of Lok Sabha. Instead, a body
constituted by Parliament would be vested with power to resolve such election disputes.

About Office of Vice President
• Second-highest constitutional office in country.
• Constitution does not specify who performs Vice President’s duties when office falls vacant.
Articles Description
Article 63 There shall be a Vice President of India.

143)
On Bills reserved for unconstitutionality, President should seek SC’s advisory
opinion on constitutionality; opinion has strong persuasive value.

of powers and duties of his/her office. However, actions of government remain open to judicial review. Article
361(2) provides absolute immunity to the Governor from criminal proceedings while in office.
1.3. OFFICE OF VICE PRESIDENT
Why in the news?
Recently, C.P. Radhakrishnan elected as the 15th Vice-President of India.
Election Procedure of Vice President:
• Nomination: Requires 20 proposers and 20 seconders; ₹15,000 deposit is mandatory.
• Voting: Conducted in Parliament House using special ECI-supplied pens. First preference vote is
compulsory.
• Supervision: ECI appoints the Returning Officer (Secretary General, Rajya Sabha) and observers.
• Key Safeguards followed in Elections:
o Provisions of the Anti-Defection Law are not applicable.

Elections Act, 1952.

Article 64 Vice President shall be ex officio chairman of Council of States (Rajya Sabha).
Article 65 Act as President or to discharge his functions during casual vacancies in office, or during
absence of President.
Article 66 Election:
• Elected by members of an electoral college which consist members of both Houses of
Parliament (both elected and nominated members)
• System of proportional representation by means of single transferable vote and secret
ballot.
• Value of every vote cast in Vice-Presidential election has same value i.e. 1.
Qualifications:
• Citizen of India.
• 35 years Age.
• He is qualified for election as a member of the Council of States (Rajya Sabha).
• Must not hold any office of profit
Article 67 • Hold office for a term of five years.
• Resign by submitting a written resignation to President.
• Removed by Rajya Sabha resolution passed by a majority of its total membership and
approved by Lok Sabha.
• Continues in office until a successor assumes charge, even after term expiry.
Article 68 • An election to fill a vacancy in the office of Vice-President occurring because of his death,
resignation or removal shall be held as soon as possible
1.4. SPEAKER OF LOK SABHA
Why in the news?
Lok Sabha Speaker has nominated a Member of Parliament (MP) as an empanelled member to preside over
House proceedings.
More on News
• Section 9 in Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha:
o At start of a session or as needed, Speaker nominates up to 10 MPs as a panel of Chairpersons.
o Any of them may preside over House in absence of Speaker and Deputy Speaker.
About Office of Speaker
• Article 93: Lok Sabha chooses two of its members as Speaker and Deputy Speaker by simple majority
of members present and voting on a date fixed by President.
• Position: Presiding officer, Head and representative of Lok Sabha.
• Term of Office:
o Does not vacate office until immediately before first meeting of Lok Sabha after dissolution.
o Eligible for re-election.
• Removal (Article 94): By a resolution passed by majority of all the then members of House, provided a
minimum of fourteen days' notice has been given before moving such a resolution.
o When a resolution for Speaker’s removal is under consideration, he cannot preside at sitting of House,
though he may be present.
o However, he can speak and take part in proceedings of House at such a time and vote in first
instance, though not in case of an equality of votes.

About Deputy Speaker (DS) of Lok Sabha
• Background: Office emerged in 1921 under Government of India Act, 1919.
o Sachidananda Sinha first held this office in Central Legislative Assembly.
o M. A. Ayyangar became first elected DS post-independence.
• Election: As per a long-standing convention, post of DS has been offered to Opposition.
o As per Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha, DS election shall be held on such
date as Speaker may fix.
• Resignation and Removal: Article 94 deals with vacation, resignation and removal (by resolution
passed by majority of all then members of House).
o Speaker may resign by submitting a resignation to DS and vice-versa.
• Duties: As per Article 95, DS performs duties of Speaker in case of vacancy, absence, etc.
o DS has same power as of Speaker when presiding over sitting of House and no appeal lies against
his rulings.

1.5. ANTI-DEFECTION LAW
Why in the news?
Supreme Court, in Padi Kaushik Reddy v.
State of Telangana (2025), recently addressed
significant delays by the Speaker in deciding
disqualification petitions concerning 10
Telangana MLAs.
About Anti-Defection Law (ADL)
• ADL provides for disqualification of MLAs
who voluntarily give up party membership
after being elected on a party ticket.
o Included under 10th schedule via
52nd Amendment Act, 1985.
> It lays down the process by which
legislators may be disqualified
on grounds of defection by the
Presiding Officer of a legislature
based on a petition by any other member of the House.
> Does not specify a time period for Presiding Officer to decide on a disqualification plea.
> Courts can intervene only after Presiding Officer has decided on matter.
Note: Original Constitution of 1950 did not mention the term “political party”. It was explicitly introduced
through Tenth Schedule.
Judicial Pronouncements in context of Tenth Schedule
• Kihoto Hollohan vs Zachillhu, 1992: Speaker's decision under anti-defection law is subject to judicial
review.
• Ravi S. Naik v. Union of India, 1994: Speaker should act as a neutral adjudicator rather than a political
figure.
• Karnataka MLAs’ disqualification case, 2020: Parliament to consider transferring disqualification
powers from Speaker to an independent tribunal.
• Keisham Meghachandra Singh v. Speaker, Manipur Legislative Assembly, 2020: Disqualification
petitions under Tenth Schedule should be decided within a period of three months from date of their filing.
1.6. DISQUALIFICATION OF A SITTING LAWMAKER
Why in the news?
A Karnataka MLA was disqualified under Article 191 of Constitution and Section 8 of RPA, 1951 after being
convicted by a CBI court in an illegal mining case, leading to a vacancy in the constituency.
Disqualification of a Sitting Lawmaker
Constitutional Provisions
• Articles 102 and 191 specify following conditions, owing to which a MP or MLA (respectively) can be
disqualified
○ Holding Office of Profit
○ Person of unsound mind (stands so declared by a competent court)
○ An undischarged insolvent
○ Not a citizen of India or has voluntarily acquired the citizenship of a foreign State
○ Defection
○ Disqualified by or under any law made by Parliament

 - Polity, Governance and Social Issues
Existing Legal and Judicial Framework for Disqualification after crimes
• RPA, 1951: Section 8(3) of RPA, legislators is disqualified from contesting elections or continuing in office
upon conviction for certain criminal offenses with imprisonment of two years or more.
o It includes conviction for two years or more in crimes like bribery, rape, promoting enmity, or
untouchability, corruption, disloyalty, etc.
• Lily Thomas v Union of India, 2013: Supreme Court ruled that lawmaker is immediately disqualified
from attracting such conviction.
• Manoj Narula v. Union of India, 2014: Court held that appointing Ministers with criminal backgrounds
are not legally barred, but advised PM to avoid selecting individuals with serious criminal antecedents.
• PIL of Public Interest Foundation, 2018: Courts cannot add new disqualification grounds.
Recommended Parliament that political parties revoke membership and deny tickets to candidate
with serious criminal cases.
1.6.1. REMOVAL OF MINISTERS UPON DETENTION
Why in the News?
Union Home Minister introduced the Constitution (One Hundred and Thirtieth Amendment) Bill, 2025 for
removal of ministers upon detention in the Lok Sabha.
Key provisions of Constitution Amendment Bill, 2025
• Grounds for Removal: Union Minister, Chief Minister, State Minister, or Prime Minister to be removed if
arrested and detained for 30 consecutive days for an offence punishable with 5 years or more
imprisonment.
• The Bills propose significant amendments to Articles 75 (provisions of Union Ministers), 164 (provisions
of State Ministers), and 239AA (Provisions for NCT Delhi) of the Indian Constitution.
Procedure for Removal:
• Union Ministers (except PM): President removes Minister on PM’s advice by the 31st day; otherwise,
automatic cessation.
• State Ministers (except CM): Governor removes on CM’s advice.
• Delhi Ministers (except CM): President acts on Delhi CM’s advice.
• Prime Minister / Chief Ministers: Must resign by 31st day of custody; failure leads to automatic removal.
• Reappointment: No bar on reappointment after release from custody.
1.7. PARLIAMENTARY COMMITTEES
Why in the news?
Recently, Lok Sabha Speaker stated that Parliamentary Committees are not adversarial to the government but
act as supportive and corrective instruments that offer constructive guidance.
About Parliamentary Committees (PCs)
• PC is appointed or elected by House or nominated by Speaker and which works under direction of
Speaker.
• It presents its report to House or Speaker.
• PCs have their origins in British Parliament.
o They draw their authority from Article 105 (deals with privileges of MPs), and Article 118 (gives
Parliament authority to make rules to regulate its procedure and conduct of business).
Types of Parliamentary Committees
• Ad hoc Committee: Appointed for specific purpose and cease to exist when they finish task assigned to
them. E.g. Railway Convention Committee etc.
• Standing Committee: Permanent and regular committees which are constituted from time to time. E.g.
Financial Committees, Committee on Subordinate legislation etc.

1.8. LOCAL BODIES
1.8.1. PANCHAYAT ADVANCEMENT INDEX (PAI)
Ministry of Panchayati Raj Introduced Panchayat Advancement Index (PAI) to Assess Gram Panchayat
Performance.
About Panchayat Advancement Index (PAI)
• Concept: PAI is a multi-domain and multi-sectoral index that is intended to be used to assess overall holistic
development, performance & progress of panchayats.
• Aims: Measure how well these grassroots institutions are achieving localized SDGs.
• Themes: index evaluates panchayats based on nine key themes related to local development like poverty
free, clean and green panchayat, socially just, healthy, etc.
• Categories in the PAI
o Achiever (0%): No panchayat in India achieved ‘achiever’ rank in this year’s assessment.
o Aspirant (61.2%): Largest percentage of panchayats falls under this category.
o Performer (36%): A significant portion of panchayats has achieved moderate progress.
o Front-Runner: Gujarat topped the rankings with the highest number of panchayats in this category.

1.8.2. MODEL YOUTH GRAM SABHA (MYGS)
Ministry of Panchayati Raj, in collaboration with the Ministry of Education and Ministry of Tribal Affairs, launched
MYGS initiative.
About MYGS
• It is a pioneering initiative to strengthen Janbhagidari and promote participatory local governance by
engaging students in simulated Gram Sabha sessions.
• It is aligned with NEP 2020, and will be implemented across Jawahar Navodaya Vidyalayas (JNVs), Eklavya
Model Residential Schools (EMRSs), and State Government Schools.
o JNVs are residential schools established under NEP 1986 to provide education to rural children
irrespective of their socio economic status.
> Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Education.
o EMRSs aim to provide quality education to Scheduled Tribes (ST) students in areas having more than
50% ST population and at least 20,000 tribal persons.
> Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Tribal Affairs
1.9. OTHER IMPORTANT NEWS
1.9.1. OFFICIAL LANGUAGE
Golden jubilee of Department of Official Language has been celebrated.
• It was established in the year 1975.
• Part of Ministry of Home Affairs.
Official Language (Part XVII of Constitution)
• Article 343: Official language of Union shall be Hindi in Devanagari script.
o Official Language Act, 1963 provides for use of English, in addition to Hindi, for all official purposes
of Union and business transactions in Parliament.
• Article 344: Constitution of Commission and Committee on Official Language.
• Article 345: State Legislature may adopt any one or more languages used in State or Hindi as official
language of that State.
1.9.2. INTER-PARLIAMENTARY UNION
Indian Lok Sabha Speaker attended the 150th Inter-Parliamentary Union (IPU) Summit in Tashkent in
Uzbekistan.
About IPU
• Genesis: Began in 1889 as a small group of parliamentarians.
• Mission: Facilitate parliamentary diplomacy and empower parliaments and parliamentarians to promote

• Article 117(3): Bills involving expenditure from Consolidated Fund of India require prior President’s

About Finance Bill and Money Bill
• Finance Bill can be introduced only in Lok Sabha. Rajya Sabha can only recommend amendments in Bill,
it cannot amend or reject.
• Bill has to be passed by Parliament within 75 days of its introduction.
• As a finance bill is a money bill so no joint sitting of two houses is allowed with regard to a finance bill
under Article 108.
o Article 109 mentions special procedure in respect of Money Bills.
o If Lok Sabha does not accept any Rajya Sabha recommendations, Money Bill is deemed to be passed by
both Houses in the form passed by Lok Sabha without any amendments recommended by Rajya Sabha.
1.10.2. PRIVATE MEMBER BILL
• About: A MP, other than a Minister (both elected and nominated) is known as a Private Member. A bill
initiated by any such Member is called a Private Member's Bill (PMB).
o Drafting of such a bill is responsibility of the Member introducing it.
• Mechanism: One month notice is given before bill’s introduction with permission of Speaker (Lok Sabha)
or Chairman (Rajya Sabha). If the bill is selected then:
o Lok Sabha allots last two-and-a-half hours of business every Friday for consideration and discussion
on the Bill.
o Rajya Sabha allots two and a half hours (from 2.30 p.m. to 5.00 p.m.) on every alternate Friday.
o First private member bill passed was Muslim Wakfs Bill, 1952 which was introduced by Syed
Mohammed Ahmad Kasmi.
o Only 14 private member bills have become law till date and the last such bill was passed in 1970.
1.10.3. ADJOURNMENT SINE DIE
• Sine Die is a Latin term meaning "without assigning a day for a further meeting."
• In parliamentary terms, it refers to end of a session of Parliament without setting a date to reconvene.
• Authority: Presiding Officer announces and later, President issues notification to prorogue House,
officially ending the session (Article 85).
• Effect on Pending Business:
o All bills that are pending in the same House remain unaffected.
o Most notices (e.g., questions, motions, resolutions) lapse and must be resubmitted in the next
session unless otherwise specified.
Note: Parliament should meet at least twice a year. There is no minimum number of days that Parliament is
required to meet in a year.
1.10.4. ZERO HOUR
• Period immediately following Question Hour and the laying of papers, before any listed business is taken
up.
• Timing: Starts around 12 noon, which is why it is termed Zero Hour.
• It is not formally recognised in the parliamentary procedure.
• Procedure for raising matters: Members must submit notices stating the subject they wish to raise.
o Speaker decides whether to allow the matter to be raised.
1.10.5. QUESTION HOUR
• First hour of every parliamentary sitting is slotted for this. During this time, members ask questions and
the ministers usually give answers.
• Three kinds of Questions:

2. IMPORTANT CONSTITUTIONAL/ STATUTORY/
EXECUTIVE BODIES IN NEWS
2.1. LOKPAL
Why in the news?
Parliamentary Standing Committee on Personnel, Public Grievances, Law and Justice suggests urgent steps to
operationalise Lokpal’s inquiry, prosecution wings in its 145th Report.
About Lokpal
• Genesis & Aim: Central, statutory, anti-corruption ombudsman established under the Lokpal &
Lokayuktas Act, 2013 to investigate and prosecute corruption involving public servants.
o Recommended by 1st and 2nd Administrative Reforms Commissions.
o Lokayukta operates at State level; structure varies across States and is appointed by the Governor
(generally in consultation with the State HC CJ and LoP).
o As per Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act 2013, power of Lokpal applies to all categories of public servants
of India, including those serving under the Union Government, whether posted within India or
abroad.
• First Lokpal: Justice Pinaki Chandra Ghosh (2019).
o Current Lokpal: Justice A.M. Khanwilkar.
• Tenure & Eligibility:
o Term: 5 years or till 70 years of age, whichever is earlier.
o No reappointment as Chairperson or Member after completion of term.
o A Member may become Chairperson only if total tenure does not exceed 5 years.

Two wings of Lokpal
Wing Key Functions & Structure
Inquiry Wing • Conducts preliminary inquiry into offences by public servants under PoCA, 1988.
• Submits inquiry report to Lokpal within 60 days.
• Headed by Director of Inquiry under Lokpal Chairperson.
• Assisted by 3 SPs (General, Economic & Banking, Cyber) and inquiry officers.
• Vested with civil court powers under CPC, 1908.
Prosecution Wing • Headed by Director of Prosecution.
• Files cases before Special Courts based on investigation reports.
• Prosecutes public servants for offences punishable under PCA, 1988.
Powers of Lokpal:
• Superintendence over Investigations: Exercises supervision and direction over central investigation
agencies, including the CBI, for cases referred by Lokpal.
• Document Protection: Can issue directions to public servants for preservation and protection of
records/documents.
• Civil Court Powers: Possesses powers under the Code of Civil Procedure to conduct preliminary inquiry
and investigation into corruption cases.
• Appellate Authority: Acts as the final appellate authority in public grievance cases involving findings of
corruption under the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.

2.4. NATIONAL CRIME RECORDS BUREAU
Why in the News?
Recently, National Crime Records Bureau (NCRB) has released Accidental Deaths & Suicides in India report.
Accidental Deaths & Suicides in India 2023 Report
• Rate of Suicide: A & N Islands (Highest) >Sikkim> Kerala.
o Cities have higher suicide rate as compared to all-India suicide rate.
• States with higher Percentage Share of Suicides: Maharashtra (Highest)>Tamil Nadu>Madhya
Pradesh> Karnataka>West Bengal
• Farmer Suicide:
o Zero suicides of Farmers/Cultivators/Agricultural Labourers: West Bengal, Bihar, Odisha,
Jharkhand etc.
Governance of Prisons in India
• Prisons and prisoners fall under State List (Entry 4, Seventh Schedule)
• Union Role: Ministry of Home Affairs provides guidance and support to States and UTs.
• Reform Initiative: Model Prisons and Correctional Services Act, 2023 issued by MHA as a guiding
framework to replace colonial-era laws; integrates provisions of Prisoners Act, 1900 and Transfer of
Prisoners Act, 1950.
Judicial Pronouncements related to Prisons
• Suhas Chakma v. Union of India & Others (2024): Supreme Court observed that establishing open
prisons is one of the solutions for overcrowding and promote rehabilitation.
• Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar (1979): Supreme Court held that right to a speedy trial is a
fundamental right under Article 21, resulting in the release of thousands of undertrial prisoners.

Why in the news?
Recently, NHRC has challenged the recommendation of Sub-committee on Accreditation (SCA) of Global
Alliance of National Human Rights Institutions (GANHRI) which proposed downgrading its status from ‘A’ to ‘B’.

Accreditation of NHRC of India (NHRCI)
• NHRCI got 'A' status of accreditation first time in 1999. In 2016, NHRCI’s accreditation was deferred by a
year after which it received an ‘A’ rating in 2017 by SCA of GANHRI.
• In 2023, accreditation of NHRCI was deferred. Current accreditation by GANHRI is deferred due to a lack
of diversity in staff and leadership and insufficient action to protect marginalised groups.
About NHRC
• Headquarters: New Delhi
• Genesis: Statutory body, established under Protection of Human Rights Act (PHRA), 1993 as amended in
2006 and in 2019.
o PHRA defines Human Rights as rights relating to life, liberty, equality and dignity of the individual. These
rights are guaranteed by Constitution or embodied in International Covenants and are enforceable by
courts in India.
• Appointment: Chairperson and Members of Commission are appointed by President of India, on
recommendations of a committee, headed by Prime Minister, consisting of:
o Speaker of Lok Sabha
o Minister-in-Charge of Ministry of Home Affairs
o Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha
o Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha
• Tenure: Chairperson and members of NHRC will hold office for three years or till the age of seventy years,
whichever is earlier.

2.6. NATIONAL STANDARDS FOR CIVIL SERVICE TRAINING INSTITUTES
(NSCSTI)
Why in the news?
Recently, NSCSTI 2.0 framework was launched by the Minister of State for Personnel, Public Grievances and
Pensions.
About National Standards for Civil Service Training Institutes
• Developed by: Capacity Building Commission (CBC) which is an executive body of Mission Karmayogi
(refer infographics) under Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances & Pensions.
• Objectives: Establish baseline capacities in Central Training Institutes, standardise civil service training,
and provide a structured framework to improve institute management.
• NSCSTI 2.0 Features:
o Hybrid and AI-driven learning models
o Inclusive design for training institutes at all levels of government
o Adoption of best practices through collaboration between public and private sectors
iGOT Karmayogi
iGOT Karmayogi crossed 1 crore registered users.
• About (2022): A comprehensive online learning platform for civil servants across India.
• Features: Offers self-paced courses, webinars, and peer learning on governance, policy,
management, and technical skills.
• Mission Link: Core component of Mission Karmayogi (National Programme for Civil Services Capacity
Building).
• Implementation: Executed by Karmayogi Bharat (SPV).
• Digital Backbone: Developed as part of the Digital India stack.

About CAG
• Constitutional Body appointed under Article 148 of Constitution by President of India.
o CAG is not eligible reappointment in central or state government.
• The extent of his duties is listed out in CAGs (Duties, Powers and Conditions of Service) Act, 1971.
o Tenure: 6 years or up to age of 65 years whichever is earlier.
o Removal: Removed by the President on the same grounds and in the same manner as a judge of the
Supreme Court.
• Other Key Detail:
o Duty of the CAG arises only after the expenditure has been incurred.
o CAG reports on the execution of projects or programs by the ministers are discussed by the Public
Accounts Committee.
o Information from CAG reports can be used by investigating agencies to press charges in court of law.
2.7.2. ATTORNEY GENERAL OF INDIA
Recently, senior advocate R. Venkataramani was re-appointed as Attorney General of India for two years.
About Attorney General of India (AGI)
• Highest law officer in country.
• Constitutional Body under Article 76 which provides for office of Attorney General for India.
• Appointment: By President.
• Eligibility: Must be a person who is qualified to be appointed a judge of Supreme Court.
o i.e. Must be a citizen of India and have been a judge of some high court for five years or an advocate
of some high court for ten years or an eminent jurist.
• Term of office: Not fixed by Constitution.
• Removal: Constitution does not contain the procedure and grounds for removal. He holds office during
pleasure of President.
• Rights of AGI:
o Right of audience in all courts in Indian territory.
o Right to speak and take part in proceedings of both Houses or their joint sitting and any committee
of Parliament of which he may be named a member, but without a right to vote.
o Enjoys all privileges and immunities that are available to a Member of Parliament.
• Limitations of AGI:
o Not advise or hold a brief against Government of India (GoI).
o Not defend accused persons in criminal prosecutions without permission of GoI.
o Not accept appointment as a director in any company or corporation without permission of GoI.
• Duties and Functions:
o Give advice to GoI upon such legal matters, which are referred to him by President.
o Appear on behalf of GoI in all cases in Supreme Court and High Court in which GoI is concerned.
o Represent GoI in any reference made by President to the Supreme Court under Article 143.
Note: Solicitor General of India is not allowed to participate in meetings of Parliament. Article 76 does not
mention about the solicitor general and additional solicitor general.

2.7.3. NATIONAL COMMISSION FOR SCHEDULED CASTES (NCSC)

About National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC)
• Constitutional body under Article 338 of the Constitution.
• Composition: Chairperson, a Vice-Chairperson, and 3 other members, appointed by the President.
• Power: It has the power of a civil court in certain matters.
• Tenure: Hold office for a term of 3 years and shall not be eligible for appointment for more than 2 terms.
• Key Functions:
o Investigate and monitor all matters relating to the safeguards provided for the SC.
o Inquire into specific complaints with respect to the deprivation of rights and safeguards of the
Scheduled Castes, etc.
2.7.4. NATIONAL MEDICAL COMMISSION (NMC)
National Medical Commission (NMC) recently completed five years.
About National Medical Commission
• Establishment: Statutory body constituted under National Medical Commission Act, 2019; came into
force in 2020.
o Replaced Medical Council of India (MCI), and the Board of Governors was dissolved.
• Commission consists of the following persons to be appointed by the Central Government, namely
o a chairperson
o ten ex officio Members
o twenty-two part-time Members
• Four Autonomous boards under NMC:
o Under- Graduate Medical Education Board
o Post- Graduate Medical Education Board
o Medical Assessment & Rating Board
o Ethics and Medical Registration Board.

3. ISSUES RELATED TO CONSTITUTION
3.1. PREVENTIVE DETENTION
Why in the news?
Recently, in Dhanya M vs State of Kerala & Others judgement, Supreme Court set aside preventive detention
under Kerala Anti-Social Activities (Prevention) Act, 2007 and held that preventive detention must not be used
as a substitute for criminal prosecution or to bypass bail orders.
Preventive Detention
• Meaning: Detention of a person without trial and conviction by a court, on a reasonable apprehension of
him doing an activity dangerous to public order.
• Parliament and State Legislatures can make laws on preventive detention under Article 22.
• Constitution gives protection against arrest and detention under Article 22 (1) and 22 (2).
o These protections are not available to a person arrested or detained under preventive detention laws
(Article 22(3)).
• Laws that permit Preventive Detention:
o Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023: Section 168 to 172 deals with the preventive action of the
police.
> No person arrested under these sections shall be detained in custody for a period exceeding twenty-
four hours from the time of his arrest unless his further detention is required under any other
provisions BNSS.
o Others:
> National Security Act, 1980;
> UAPA, 1967;
> Maintenance of Internal Security Act (MISA), 1971
> Conservation of Foreign Exchange and Prevention of Smuggling Activities Act (COFEPOSA), 1974;
> Narcotic Drug and Psychotropic Substance Act (NDPS), 1985
> Prevention of Black marketing and Maintenance of Supplies of Essential Commodities Act
(PBMSECA), 1980 etc.
• Grounds for Preventive Detention: Security of State; Foreign affairs or Security of India; Maintenance of
Public Order; Maintenance of supplies and essential services and defence.
Constitutional Provisions related to Preventive Detention
• Article 22 (1): Right to be informed of arrest grounds
• Article 22 (2): Presentation before magistrate within 24 hours and detention beyond requires
magistrate’s approval
• Article 22 (4): Detention beyond 3 months requires advisory board approval
• Article 22 (5): Early notification of grounds for detention and opportunity to contest detention order.
Judicial Pronouncements in context of Preventive Detention
• A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras, 1950: Supreme Court upheld constitutional validity of Preventive
Detention Act, 1950 because of presence of explicit provisions of Article 22(5).
• Vijay Narayan Singh v State of Bihar, 1984: Preventive detention should not be used to circumvent
regular criminal procedures.
• Rekha v. State of Tamil Nadu, 2011: Preventive detention is an exception to Article 21 and should be
applied rarely.
• Jaseela Shaji vs Union of India, 2024: If there is failure or even delay in furnishing documents relied on
for such detention, it would amount to denial of right to make an effective representation under Article
22(5).

3.2. PERSONALITY RIGHTS
Why in the news?
Recently, Delhi High Court protected Personality Rights of Sadhguru Jaggi Vasudev from being misused via AI by
websites and platforms.
About Personality Rights
• Definition: Right of an individual to control unauthorized use of their personal attributes like Name, Image,
voice, Likeness and Distinctive expressions or traits.
o These rights include both commercial and non-commercial aspects.
• Not expressly mentioned in any statute in India.
• Components of Personality Rights:
o Right to publicity: Right to protect one’s image and likeness from being commercially exploited
without permission.
> Partially and indirectly governed by statutes like Trademarks Act, 1999 and Copyright Act, 1957.
o Right to privacy: Right to not have one’s personality represented publicly without permission.
> Governed under Article 21 and through Supreme Court judgment in Justice K.S. Puttaswamy
(Retd.) Case (2017).
• Posthumous Personality Rights in India: No specific statutory recognition protecting Posthumous
Personality Rights.
o However, Emblems Act, 1950 prohibits commercial use of images of Mahatma Gandhi and Prime
Minister.
o Deepa Jayakumar v. AL Vijay (2019) Judgment: Personality right, reputation or privacy enjoyed by a
person during his lifetime comes to an end after his or her lifetime.
Judicial pronouncements on Personality rights in India
• ICC Development (International) Ltd. vs. Arvee Enterprises, 2003 (Delhi HC): Any effort to take away
publicity right from individuals would be violative of Articles 19 and 21.
• Arun Jaitley vs Network Solutions Private Limited and Ors case, 2011 (Delhi HC): Popularity or fame
of an individual will be no different on internet than in reality.
• Rajinikanth v. Varsha Productions (Madras HC, 2015): Using a celebrity’s name, image, or style without
consent violates personality rights.
3.3. PHONE TAPPING
Why in the news?
Recently, Madras High Court has held that an individual's phone cannot be tapped in a secret operation to detect
the commission of a crime, and the same would violate the individual's fundamental right to privacy.
• Laws on phone tapping in India:
o Post Office Act, 2023: Allows interception of communication through post.
o Telecommunications (Procedures and Safeguards for Lawful Interception of Messages) Rules,
2024: Issued under the newly enacted Telecommunications Act, 2023, lay down the procedure and due
process for the government to lawfully intercept message.
o Indian Telegraph Act, 1885: Central and State Governments have right to tap phones. In states,
police have powers to tap phones.
 At Centre, 10 agencies are authorized to do so: IB, CBI, ED, Narcotics Control Bureau, CBDT, DRI,
NIA, R&AW, Directorate of Signal Intelligence, and Delhi Police Commissioner.
✓ Tapping by any other agency would be considered illegal.
 Grounds for tapping like Sovereignty, security of state, etc, are provided under Section 5(2)
(refer infographic on Supreme Court Ruling).

✓ Exception for Press: Press messages accredited to Central or State Government shall not be
intercepted unless their transmission has been prohibited.
 Orders issued only by Secretary, Union Ministry of Home Affairs or his State Counterpart in
writing; only then tapping can begin.
 Exceptional cases: Order may be issued by lower authorities also. Such order has to be
communicated to competent authority within a specified time period.
o Information Technology Act, 2000: Governs interception of WhatsApp messages, emails, etc.
3.4. CONSTITUTIONAL VALIDITY OF NARCO TESTS
Why in the news?
Recently, Supreme Court overturned Patna High Court’s order which allowed narco-tests on all accused and
witnesses.
About Narco-Analysis Test
• Interrogation technique in which a suspect is administered a psychoactive drug to suppress reasoning and
self-control.
• Drug used is sodium pentothal, which is also used in higher dosages for inducing general anesthesia in
surgeries.
Key Highlights of Judgement
Judgment draws from Selvi V. State of Karnataka (2010), which examined constitutional validity of narco-
analysis and polygraph tests.
• Involuntary Narco Test Contravenes Constitution:
o Such tests violate Articles 20(3) (right against self-incrimination) and 21 (right to personal liberty).
o Forced narco-tests are illegal and information obtained from involuntary test is inadmissible in
court.
• Voluntary Narco-Test not sufficient for Conviction:
o Even if done voluntarily, narco-analysis reports cannot be used directly as evidence.
o However, facts discovered may be admissible under Indian Evidence Act, 1872.
o This has also been reiterated by Supreme Court in Vinobhai v. State of Kerala and Manoj Kumar Soni
v. State of M.P. cases.
• Limited Right to Voluntarily Narco-Test: Accused does not have an absolute right to undergo narco-
analysis.
o It may be permitted voluntarily at an appropriate stage i.e., when accused is exercising his right to lead
evidence in a trial.

3.5. ISSUES RELATED TO ARTICLE 21
3.5.1. INCLUSIVE DIGITAL ACCESS PART OF ARTICLE 21: SUPREME COURT
Recently, Supreme Court in Amar Jain vs Union of India and Ors judgment held that inclusive and meaningful
digital access to e-governance and welfare delivery systems is a part of fundamental right to life and liberty.
Key Highlights of Judgment
• Directed to revise digital KYC norms: To enable
persons with facial disfiguration due to acid attacks or
visual impairment to access banking and e-governance
services.
o Under Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act,
2016, Court issued twenty directions to make
eKYC process accessible to them.
• Invoking ‘principle of substantive equality’: Digital
transformation must be both inclusive and equitable.
• Part of Article 21: Right to digital access emerges as an
instinctive component of the right to life and liberty.
• State's obligation: Under Articles 21 (Right to a dignified life), 14 (Right to Equality), 15 (Right against
discrimination), and 38 (directs State to promote Social Justice) of Constitution, state is obliged to ensure
digital infrastructure to all vulnerable marginalized populations.
Related news: Right to Rehabilitation
• In a recent judgement (Estate Officer, Haryana Urban Development Authority vs landowners), Supreme
Court claimed that Rehabilitation is not a fundamental right.
• It further stated that Deprivation of livelihood under Article 21 is not a sustainable ground in land
acquisition cases
3.6. OTHER IMPORTANT NEWS
3.6.1. RESERVATION OF SEATS FOR SCHEDULED TRIBES
Lok Sabha passed a bill to provide Scheduled Tribe (ST) reservation in the Goa Legislative Assembly.
About Reservation of Seats for Scheduled Tribes
• Reservation for SCs/STs: Article 332 mandates seat reservation for Scheduled Castes (SCs) and STs in
legislative assemblies of all states (except STs in Assam's autonomous districts).
• Special Provision for Assam: Seats shall be reserved for the autonomous districts in the Legislative
Assembly.
• Proportional Representation: Reserved seats must reflect proportion of SC/ST population in state.
3.6.2. ENEMY PROPERTIES ACT 1968
Madhya Pradesh High Court dismissed an actor’s objection to government notification for notifying his property
under Enemy properties Act 1968.
Enemy Property Act, 1968
• Defines ‘enemy’ as a country (its citizens) that committed external aggression against India (i.e., Pakistan
and China).
• Enemy Property means any property for the time being belonging to or held or managed on behalf of an
enemy, an enemy subject or an enemy firm.
• It vests these assets to Custodian of Enemy Property for India, Ministry of home affairs.
• Enemy Property (Amendment and Validation) Act, 2017 expanded definition of enemy to include:

- Polity, Governance and Social Issues
3.5. ISSUES RELATED TO ARTICLE 21
3.5.1. INCLUSIVE DIGITAL ACCESS PART OF ARTICLE 21: SUPREME COURT
Recently, Supreme Court in Amar Jain vs Union of India and Ors judgment held that inclusive and meaningful
digital access to e-governance and welfare delivery systems is a part of fundamental right to life and liberty.
Key Highlights of Judgment
• Directed to revise digital KYC norms: To enable
persons with facial disfiguration due to acid attacks or
visual impairment to access banking and e-governance
services.
o Under Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act,
2016, Court issued twenty directions to make
eKYC process accessible to them.
• Invoking ‘principle of substantive equality’: Digital
transformation must be both inclusive and equitable.
• Part of Article 21: Right to digital access emerges as an
instinctive component of the right to life and liberty.
• State's obligation: Under Articles 21 (Right to a dignified life), 14 (Right to Equality), 15 (Right against
discrimination), and 38 (directs State to promote Social Justice) of Constitution, state is obliged to ensure
digital infrastructure to all vulnerable marginalized populations.
Related news: Right to Rehabilitation
• In a recent judgement (Estate Officer, Haryana Urban Development Authority vs landowners), Supreme
Court claimed that Rehabilitation is not a fundamental right.
• It further stated that Deprivation of livelihood under Article 21 is not a sustainable ground in land
acquisition cases
3.6. OTHER IMPORTANT NEWS
3.6.1. RESERVATION OF SEATS FOR SCHEDULED TRIBES
Lok Sabha passed a bill to provide Scheduled Tribe (ST) reservation in the Goa Legislative Assembly.
About Reservation of Seats for Scheduled Tribes
• Reservation for SCs/STs: Article 332 mandates seat reservation for Scheduled Castes (SCs) and STs in
legislative assemblies of all states (except STs in Assam's autonomous districts).
• Special Provision for Assam: Seats shall be reserved for the autonomous districts in the Legislative
Assembly.
• Proportional Representation: Reserved seats must reflect proportion of SC/ST population in state.
3.6.2. ENEMY PROPERTIES ACT 1968
Madhya Pradesh High Court dismissed an actor’s objection to government notification for notifying his property
under Enemy properties Act 1968.
Enemy Property Act, 1968
• Defines ‘enemy’ as a country (its citizens) that committed external aggression against India (i.e., Pakistan
and China).
• Enemy Property means any property for the time being belonging to or held or managed on behalf of an
enemy, an enemy subject or an enemy firm.
• It vests these assets to Custodian of Enemy Property for India, Ministry of home affairs.
• Enemy Property (Amendment and Validation) Act, 2017 expanded definition of enemy to include:

In State of Jharkhand v. Rukma Kesh Mishra, Supreme Court clarified that Article 311(1) does not require

dismissal.
About Article 311
• Deals with dismissal, removal, or reduction in rank of persons employed in civil capacities under the
Union or State.
• Article 311 (1): Members of Civil service cannot be dismissed or removed by any authority subordinate
to the appointing authority.
• Article 311 (2): Members of Civil Service can be dismissed, removed or reduced in rank after according
him/her a reasonable opportunity of being heard post an inquiry.
Note: Provisions under Article 311 are enforceable in a court of law.

4. JUDICIARY
4.1. CHIEF JUSTICE OF INDIA
Why in the news?
Justice BR Gavai took oath as the 52nd Chief Justice of India (CJI).
4.1.1. APPOINTMENT AND REMOVAL OF JUDGES
Why in the news?
Recently, two new judge appointments have brought Supreme Court to full bench strength of 34 judges.
About Appointment of Judges
• The process of appointing a judge of Supreme Court is governed by Collegium system, which was
established through a series of Supreme Court judgments.
Collegium system evolved with Three Judges Case
• First Judges Case, 1981 or S P Gupta Case: Supreme Court ruled that recommendation made by CJI to
President can be refused for “cogent reasons”, thereby giving greater say to executive.
• Second Judges Case, 1993 (Supreme Court Advocates on Record Association (SCARA) vs Union of
India): CJI only need to consult two senior-most judges over judicial appointments and transfers.
• Third Judges Case, 1998: CJIs should consult with four senior-most Supreme Court judges to form his
opinion on judicial appointments and transfers.

About Removal of Judges
• Article 124(4): Deals with removal of judges of Supreme Court on grounds of proven misbehavior and
incapacity.
o However, the Constitution does not define these grounds.
• Article 124(5): Deals with power of Parliament to regulate procedure for presentation of an address and
for investigation and proof of misbehavior or incapacity of a Judge under clause (4).
o Procedure is regulated by Judges Enquiry Act (1968). (Enacted under article 124(5)).
o Special Majority Required: Majority of total membership of that House and by not less than two-thirds
of total members of that House present and voting.
• Article 217(1)(b): Deals with removal of a Judge of a High Court.
o It states that a High Court Judge may be removed from his office by President in the manner provided in
Article 124 (4) for removal of a Judge of Supreme Court.
• Article 218: Extends Applicability of Article 124 (4) and Article 124 (5) to High courts.
• No Judge has been removed until now.
• Constitution does not use the term impeachment for removal of judges, it is colloquially used to refer to
proceedings under Article 124 and Article 218. Judges Enquiry Act (1968) regulates procedure relating to
removal of Supreme Court judge by process of impeachment.

Additional Judges
Recently, the President appointed 14 Advocates as Additional Judges of Bombay High Court.
About Additional Judges
• Appointed under: Article 224
• Time Period: Not exceeding two years and shall hold office till the age of 62 years.
• Reason: Any temporary increase in business of a High Court or by reason of arrears of work.
• Sanctioned Strength: Permanent and Additional Judges in the ratio of 75:25.
District Judges
Supreme Court held that judicial officers with combined experience of 7 years as an advocate and in
service will be eligible for direct recruitment as District Judges.
• It held that Article 233 permitted both advocates and in-service candidates to be appointed as District
Judges and extended the criteria of 7 years’ practice to judicial officers as well.
Appointment of District Judge
• Article 233 deals with appointment of District Judge.
• Appointment Authority: Appointed by the Governor in consultation with the High Court

4.3. LEGAL AID IN INDIA
Why in the news?
Recently, the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987, implemented on November 9, 1995 completed 30 years of its
implementation.
About Legal Services Act 1987
• Came into force in 1995, it established a three-tier system to provide free and competent legal services.
o National Legal Services Authority (NALSA): Headed by Chief Justice of India.
o State Legal Services Authorities (SLSA): Headed by Chief Justice of High Court.
o District Legal Services Authorities (DLSA): Headed by District Judge.
• Section 341 of Bhartiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023: Legal aid to accused at State expense in certain
cases where accused lacks the means for legal engagement.
• Government Steps taken for legal aid: Designing Innovative Solutions for Holistic Access to Justice in India
(DISHA) scheme; Tele-Law; Nyaya Mitra programme; Lok Adalat etc.
Note: Free legal services also include providing aid and advice to help beneficiaries access welfare schemes
of Central or State Government and to ensure their overall access to justice.
National Legal Services Authority (NALSA)
• It is a statutory body constituted under LSA Act, 1987 to provide free and competent legal services to
weaker sections of the society.
• Functions:
o Formulate policies, principles, guidelines and frame effective schemes for SLSAs to implement
Legal Services Programmes throughout country.
o Monitor and evaluate implementation of legal aid programmes.
o Organize Lok Adalats for amicable settlement of disputes.

4.4.1. COURTS POWERS TO MODIFY ARBITRAL AWARDS
Supreme Court recently held that courts have limited powers to modify arbitral awards.
Key highlights of Judgement
• In Gayatri Balasamy v. ISG Novasoft Technologies Ltd., the Supreme Court held that courts can modify
arbitral awards under Sections 34 and 37 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.
• Such modification is permissible when:
o The award is severable, allowing invalid parts to be separated from valid ones (doctrine of omne majus
continet in se minus).
o There are clerical, computational, or typographical errors.
o Post-award interest requires modification in appropriate cases.
4.4.2. LOK ADALAT
Kerala becomes first State in India to launch Online Permanent Lok Adalat Services.
About Lok Adalat (People’s Court)
• Lok Adalats is one of the Alternative Dispute Redressal Mechanisms.
• Establishment: Accorded statutory status under Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987.
o In 2002, Act was amended to establish permanent Lok Adalats for public utility services, which are
guided by principles of natural justice.
• Powers: They are not bound by Code of Civil Procedure (CPC), 1908 and Indian Evidence Act 1872.
o It has same powers as a civil court under CPC, 1908.
o Decision made by Lok Adalats is final and binding and no appeal can be made against its decision.
• Cases referred to Lok Adalat includes
o Any case pending before any court,
o Any dispute which has not been brought before any court and is likely to be filed before court.
• Nature of Cases undertaken: Includes matrimonial disputes, property disputes, motor accident
claims, land acquisition etc.
• First Lok Adalat: Held in 1982 at Junagarh, Gujarat.
4.5. KEY TERMS/INITIATIVES IN NEWS
4.5.1. DOCTRINE OF MERGER
The Supreme Court (SC) has recently clarified the ‘Doctrine of Merger’ not of rigid & universal application.
About Doctrine of Merger
• Rationale: there cannot be more than one operative decree at a given point of time.
• Application: It applies irrespective of whether the appellate court has affirmed, modified or reversed the
decree of the trial court.
o It means that after a higher court gives its verdict, the lower court’s decision becomes part of that
ruling and is no longer treated as a standalone judgment.
• Purpose and Benefits: maintenance of the decorum of the hierarchy of courts and tribunals, ensures
finality in judicial decisions
• In State of Madras v. Madurai Mills Co. Ltd., Supreme Court held that the doctrine of merger applies
depending on the nature of the appellate or revisional order and the scope of the statutory provisions
granting such jurisdiction.

4.5.2. E-ZERO FIR
MHA's Indian Cybercrime Coordination Centre (I4C) has introduced the new e-Zero FIR initiative.
About e-Zero FIR
• Launched as a pilot project for Delhi.
• Aim: ease reporting and prompt action on complaints related to cyber financial crimes.
• Threshold: Financial losses above threshold limit of Rs. 10 lakh made to National Cybercrime Reporting
Portal (NCRP) or 1930 helpline will automatically lead to registration of a Zero FIR with e-Crime Police
Station of Delhi.
o This will be immediately routed to the territorial Cyber Crime Police Stations.
o Complainants can visit cybercrime Police Station within 3 days and get the Zero FIR converted into
a regular FIR.
About Zero FIR
• Zero FIR is the mode of logging FIR in any Police Station irrespective of the offence committed in that area
or any other area of jurisdiction.
o The provision for Zero FIR is under Section 173 (1) of Bharatiya Nagrik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, based
on Justice Verma Committee's recommendation established after 2012 Nirbhaya case.

5. ELECTIONS
Judicial Pronouncements for Reforming India’s Election Process
• Association for Democratic Reforms (ADR) v. Union of India (2002): Mandated candidates to disclose
criminal, financial, and educational details, enhancing voter transparency.
• People’s Union for Civil Liberties (PUCL) v. Union of India (2013): Introduced NOTA option on EVMs;
empowering fundamental right of choice (Article 19(1)(a)).
• Lily Thomas v. Union of India (2013): Struck down Section 8(4) of RPA, 1951, ensuring immediate
disqualification of convicted legislators.
• ADR v. Union of India (2024): Declared Electoral Bonds Scheme unconstitutional, restoring
transparency in political funding.
5.1. REGISTERED UNRECOGNIZED POLITICAL PARTIES (RUPP)
Why in the news?
ECI has identified another 476 RUPP for possible de-listing as part of its process to clean up the electoral
system.
About Registered Unrecognized Political Parties (RUPP)
• RUPPs are political parties registered with the ECI but lack recognition as State or National parties.
• Legal Provisions & Guidelines
o Section 29A of RPA, 1951 specifies the criteria that a political party must register itself with the ECI.
o ECI lacks the authority to de-register any political party under RPA as established in case of Indian
National Congress versus Institute of Social Welfare (2002).

5.2. SPECIAL INTENSIVE REVISION
Why in the news?
Recently, ECI has published the draft electoral rolls following the Special Intensive Revision (SIR) in West Bengal,
Rajasthan, Goa, Lakshadweep and Puducherry.
About Special Intensive Revision
• It is legally backed by Article 324 of Constitution and Section 21(3) of RPA, 1950.
• It is a House-to-House verification done by Booth Level Officers to ensure enrolment of all eligible
Citizens in Electoral Roll.
o SIR was conducted eight times from 1951 till 2004, with last exercise undertaken over 21 years ago.
Electoral Rolls
• Electoral roll is a list of persons registered as electors residing in a constituency.
• Article 326 of the Constitution, and Section 19 of Representation of the People Act, 1950 stipulate that
the minimum age for registration of a voter as 18 years.
o Through the 61st Amendment Act, 1988 the minimum age of registration of a voter was brought
down to 18 years from 21.
5.3. OTHER IMPORTANT NEWS
5.3.1. APPOINTMENT AND REMOVAL OF CHIEF ELECTION COMMISSIONER (CEC)
CEC, ECs and their Appointment is done under Chief Election Commissioner and Other Election Commissioner
Act, 2023 (refer infographic).
• The 2023 Act provides more autonomy to the ECI as under the Act:
o CEC and ECs enjoy Supreme Court judge-level salary & benefits.
o Granted immunity from civil/criminal proceedings for official duties.
o Search & selection committees for appointments.
o Fixed tenure for CEC & ECs.
Process for Removal (as mentioned under Article 124(4))
• Article 324(5) states that CEC can be removed from office only “in like manner and on like grounds as a
Judge of the Supreme Court.”
• Thus, removal of CEC begins with a motion in either House alleging misbehaviour or incapacity.
o Once the motion is admitted, a committee enquires into validity of the charges.

5.4. KEY TERMS/INITIATIVES IN NEWS
5.4.1. ECINET DIGITAL PLATFORM
ECI used the new ECINET digital platform during recent bye-elections in five Assembly constituencies.
About ECINET app
• One-stop digital platform for timely voter turnout reporting.
o Under Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961 (framed under RPA, 1951), Presiding Officers must provide
Form 17C to booth-level agents after polls.
o Form 17C records polling station wise voter turnout details (electors, votes cast/denied/not recorded)
and candidate-wise total votes polled.
 Its Rule 49B revised to improve EVM ballot paper readability, including colour photographs of
candidates.
• Key Features
o Presiding Officers update voter turnout data every two hours on polling day.
o Only designated EC officials can enter data to ensure accuracy.
o Faster Index Card Publication in 72 hours of results.
 Index Cards are non-statutory post-election reports providing constituency-level data on
candidates, voters, votes, party shares, gender voting patterns, and regional variations.
5.4.2. POSTAL BALLOT
According to ECI’s notification, penultimate round of EVM counting shall be taken up only after complete
counting of Postal Ballots.

About Postal Ballot
• Allows voters to cast their votes on an electronically received postal ballot.
• Legal Framework: Governed by Conduct of Election Rules, 1961 and RPA, 1951.
• Postal Ballot embeds 3 exceptions: Polling takes place outside Polling Station, it is by non-EVM
methodology, and it takes place prior to the date of poll fixed.
• Issue of ballot paper: A postal ballot paper shall be sent by post under certificate of posting to elector
together with a declaration in Form 13A; a cover in Form 13B; a large cover addressed to the returning officer
in Form 13C; and instructions for the guidance of the elector in Form 13D.

6. CENTRE-STATE RELATIONS
6.1. STATES’ DEMAND FOR AUTONOMY
Why in the News?
Tamil Nadu government has formed a high-level committee, headed by former Supreme Court judge Justice
Kurian Joseph, to suggest ways to strengthen State autonomy and federalism.
Federal Scheme of Indian Constitution
• India is a union of states, with no right to secede.
o It has common features such as single
constitution, single citizenship, all-
India services, election commission
and single unified judiciary.
• Division of Legislative Powers
o Article 246: Distributes powers
between Parliament and States through
Seventh Schedule.
> Union list, State list; Concurrent
list.
> Any constitutional amendment
affecting Lists in Seventh
Schedule must be ratified by at least half of the State legislatures.
• Indian federalism is quasi-federal with a stronger Centre and gives it more power than States.
6.1.1. REORGANISATION OF STATE
Recently, Uttarakhand marked 25 years of its formation.
Procedure for Formation of New States
• Article 3: Provisions for formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries or names of existing
States. As per this,
o Power: Parliament may by law form a new State.
o Presidential Recommendation: Such Bill shall be introduced in either House of Parliament only on
President’s recommendation.
o Consultation with State Legislatures: Before recommending a Bill, The President shall refer this Bill
to Legislature of that State to express its view within a specified time.
• Parliament can create a new state through ordinary legislation (i.e. with simple majority).
Constitutional Provisions related to Federalism
• Article 248: Residuary Powers rest with Parliament
• Article 249: Laws on State subjects for national interest
• Article 250: Centre can legislate on state list items
during emergencies.
• Article 252: Parliament makes laws for States with their
consent.
• Article 253: International treaties override State
powers.
• Article 254: Union law prevails in case of conflict.

Judicial Pronouncement related to Reorganisation of State
Berubari Union case, 1960: Supreme Court held that power of Parliament to diminish the area of State (under
Article 3) does not cover cession of Indian Territory to a foreign country.
• Indian Territory can be ceded to a foreign state only by amending Constitution under Article 368.
• Consequently, 9th Constitutional Amendment Act (CAA), 1960 was enacted to transfer certain territory
to Pakistan.
6.2. FISCAL FEDERALISM
Why in the news?
Former RBI Governor Duvvuri Subbarao underscored the need for a cooperative approach to India’s fiscal
federalism.
Basis of Devolution (15th Finance Commission recommendations)
• Vertical Devolution: States share from divisible pool stands at 41% based on recommendations of 15th
FC.
• Horizontal Devolution: As per 15th FC, assigned criteria with their weights are: Income Distance (45%),
Area (15%), Population, 2011 (15%), Demographic performance (12.5), Forest and Ecology (10%), and
Tax and fiscal efforts (2.5%).
• Education: Recommended grants of Rs. 4,800 crore (Rs. 1,200 crore each year) from 2022-23 to 2025-
26 for incentivising the States to enhance educational outcomes.
• Agriculture: Recommended that Rs. 45,000 crore be kept as performance-based incentive for all the
States for carrying out agricultural reforms.
• Total: Aggregate transfers to States is estimated to remain at around 50.9 per cent of the divisible pool
during 2021-26 period
About Finance Commission
• Constitutional body established under Article 280 by President every five years.
• Functions: Make recommendations regarding devolution of taxes between Center and States from
divisible pool which includes all central taxes excluding surcharges and cess.
o Review state of finances of Union and States.

Other measures taken to resolve ISWD
• Inter-State River Water Disputes (Amendment) Bill, 2019
o Disputes Resolution Committee to resolve inter-state disputes amicably.
o Establishment of single Inter-State River Water Disputes Tribunal with multiple benches.
o Strict timeline for completion of award.
o Data bank at the central level for each river basin.
• Draft River Basin Management Bill, 2018
o Objective: The bill proposes principled approach of participation, cooperation, equitable and
sustainable use of water, integrated river-basin management etc.
o Creating River Basin Master Plan
o Establishment of River Basin Authority
• National River Linking Project: Transfer water from surplus region to deficit region potentially reducing
inter-state water disputes.
Note: While the Supreme Court cannot adjudicate the original dispute, it can interpret the decisions of a
tribunal, and may direct parties back to the tribunal for further clarification if needed.

7. IMPORTANT LEGISLATIONS/BILLS
7.1. DIGITAL PERSONAL DATA PROTECTION (DPDP) ACT, 2023
Why in the news?
Recently, Union Government has notified large parts of the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023. It
has also notified Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Rules, 2025.
About Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act 2023
• It aims to regulate the processing of digital personal data while ensuring individuals' right to protect
their data.
• It protects digital personal data by providing:
o Obligations of Data Fiduciaries (persons, companies and government entities who process data) for
data processing (that is, collection, storage or any other operation on personal data);
o The rights and duties of Data Principals (that is, the person to whom the data relates);
o Financial penalties for breach of rights, duties, and obligations.
Key Provisions of the Act
Specification Key Details
Applicability • Applies to digital personal data processed in India (digital or digitised).
• Applies to processing outside India if offering goods/services in India.
• Excludes personal-purpose data and data made publicly available by the Data
Principal or under legal obligation.
Consent • Data processed only for lawful purpose with consent, withdrawable anytime.
• Consent not required for legitimate uses (government benefits, medical
emergencies, etc.).
• For children or persons with disability, consent by parent/legal guardian.
Data Protection
Board of India
(DPBI)
• Established by Central Government.
• Functions: monitor compliance, impose penalties, order remedial measures,
hear grievances.
• Members appointed for 2 years, eligible for reappointment.
• Appeals lie with TDSAT.
Rights & Duties of
Data Principal
• Rights: access information, correction & erasure, grievance redressal,
nomination of representative.
• Duty: no false/frivolous complaints or false particulars.
• Penalty for violation: up to ₹10,000.
Obligations of Data
Fiduciaries
• Ensure accuracy and completeness of data.
• Implement reasonable security safeguards.
• Inform DPBI and affected persons in case of breach.
• Erase data once purpose is met unless legally required.
Significant Data
Fiduciaries (SDF)
• Notified by Centre based on data volume/sensitivity, risks to rights, sovereignty,
security, electoral democracy, public order.
• Additional duties: Data Protection Officer, independent auditor, impact
assessments.
Children’s Data
Protection
• Prohibits processing harmful to child’s well-being.
• No tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising for children.
Cross-border Data
Transfer
• Permitted except to countries restricted by the government.
Penalties • Up to ₹200 crore for child-related violations.
• Up to ₹250 crore for failure to prevent data breaches.

7.1.1. DIGITAL PERSONAL DATA PROTECTION (DPDP) RULES, 2025
Implementation timeline: 18-month phased compliance period for organizations to adjust their systems and
adopt responsible data practices.
Key Provisions of the rules
• Obligations on Data Fiduciaries
o Issue clear, standalone consent notices with
itemised data details and withdrawal
mechanism.
o Erase personal data once purpose is served,
unless legally required to retain it.
> Processing logs must be retained for
minimum 1 year, unless extended by law.
o Notify personal data breaches promptly to
affected individuals with remedial steps.
o Publish contact details of designated
officer/DPO on website or app.
o Respond within 90 days to requests for access,
correction, updation or erasure.
o Children’s data protection through verifiable parental/guardian consent.
> Consent exemption allowed for essential services like healthcare, education, and child safety.
• Data Protection Board of India (DPBI)
o Comprises 4 members with a dedicated portal and mobile app for complaint filing and tracking.
• Other Provisions
o Section 44(3) of the DPDP Act, 2023 Revises Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act: By exempting disclosure of
information which relates to personal information.
> The blanket exemption of and the vague definition of personal information provides discretion to
public authorities.
o Enhanced obligations on SDFs, including periodic DPIA and independent audits every 12 months.
o Consent Managers must be companies based in India.
o Special safeguards for persons with disabilities unable to make legal decisions.
o Any personal data processed by a Data Fiduciary may be transferred outside the territory of India
subject to the restriction by the Central Government.
7.2. CRIMINAL LAW REFORM ACTS
Why in the news?
Three new Criminal Laws which came into effect on 1st July 2024 has completed one year.
7.2.1. BHARATIYA NYAYA SANHITA 2023
It replaces the Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860, the principal law on criminal offences in India.
Aspect Key Points
Background • Before IPC 1860: Laws included Parliamentary Charters, EIC regulations, Hindu
& Muslim law, and customary laws.
• Draft Penal Code (1837) prepared under T.B. Macaulay.
• IPC enacted in 1860 after the 1857 Revolt.
Punishments • Introduces community service as punishment for petty offences.
Sexual Offences • Age for gang rape victim classified as major raised from 16 to 18 years.

7.1.1. DIGITAL PERSONAL DATA PROTECTION (DPDP) RULES, 2025
Implementation timeline: 18-month phased compliance period for organizations to adjust their systems and
adopt responsible data practices.
Key Provisions of the rules
• Obligations on Data Fiduciaries
o Issue clear, standalone consent notices with
itemised data details and withdrawal
mechanism.
o Erase personal data once purpose is served,
unless legally required to retain it.
Processing logs must be retained for
minimum 1 year, unless extended by law.
o Notify personal data breaches promptly to
affected individuals with remedial steps.
o Publish contact details of designated
officer/DPO on website or app.
o Respond within 90 days to requests for access,
correction, updation or erasure.
o Children’s data protection through verifiable parental/guardian consent.
Consent exemption allowed for essential services like healthcare, education, and child safety.
• Data Protection Board of India (DPBI)
o Comprises 4 members with a dedicated portal and mobile app for complaint filing and tracking.
• Other Provisions
o Section 44(3) of the DPDP Act, 2023 Revises Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act: By exempting disclosure of
information which relates to personal information.
The blanket exemption of and the vague definition of personal information provides discretion to
public authorities.
o Enhanced obligations on SDFs, including periodic DPIA and independent audits every 12 months.
o Consent Managers must be companies based in India.
o Special safeguards for persons with disabilities unable to make legal decisions.
o Any personal data processed by a Data Fiduciary may be transferred outside the territory of India
subject to the restriction by the Central Government.
7.2. CRIMINAL LAW REFORM ACTS
Why in the news?
Three new Criminal Laws which came into effect on 1st July 2024 has completed one year.
7.2.1. BHARATIYA NYAYA SANHITA 2023
It replaces the Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860, the principal law on criminal offences in India.
Aspect Key Points
Background • Before IPC 1860: Laws included Parliamentary Charters, EIC regulations, Hindu
& Muslim law, and customary laws.
• Draft Penal Code (1837) prepared under T.B. Macaulay.
• IPC enacted in 1860 after the 1857 Revolt.
Punishments • Introduces community service as punishment for petty offences.
Sexual Offences • Age for gang rape victim classified as major raised from 16 to 18 years.
• Criminalises sexual intercourse obtained through deceit or false promise.

7.2.2. BHARATIYA NAGARIK SURAKSHA SANHITA 2023
It replaces the Criminal Procedure Code, 1973 (CrPC), which provided for the procedure for arrest,
prosecution, and bail under various Acts.
Aspect Key Points
Background • First enacted in 1861; revised Codes in 1872 and 1882.
• Major amendments in 1898, 1923, 1955.
• CrPC, 1973 enacted based on 41st Law Commission Report.
Detention of Undertrials • First-time offender released on bond after completing 1/3rd of maximum
sentence.
• Bail mandatory if 1/2 of maximum sentence completed.
• Not applicable to offences punishable with death, life imprisonment, or
multiple pending cases.
Medical Examination Police may seek medical examination of accused, including in rape cases.
Forensic Investigation Mandatory for offences punishable with 7 years or more imprisonment.
Specimen Collection Magistrate empowered to order signatures, fingerprints, and voice samples.
Procedural Timelines Prescribes timelines for medical reports, investigation progress updates to
victims, framing of charges, and pronouncement of judgments.
7.2.3. BHARATIYA SAKSHYA ADHINIYAM 2023
It replaces Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (IEA), which governed the admissibility of evidence in Indian Courts in all
civil and criminal proceedings.
7.3. PROMOTION AND REGULATION OF ONLINE GAMING ACT, 2025
Why in the News?
The Parliament has passed Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025 effectively banning online
money games while promoting and regulating other kinds of online games.
About Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025
• Establishes a legal framework to regulate and promote online gaming, fostering innovation, economic
growth, and a safe, responsible digital environment.
• Introduced as a Finance Bill under Articles 117(1) and 117(3) with President’s recommendation.
Sedition
Replaced
(Section 152)
• Sedition removed; penalises acts threatening sovereignty such as Secession,
armed rebellion, subversive activities, promoting separatism, threats to unity
and integrity of India.
Organised Crime • Defined as continuing unlawful activity including kidnapping, extortion,
contract killing, land grabbing, cybercrime, etc.
Group-based
Violence
• Murder by a group on grounds of race, caste, community, sex, language, belief,
etc.: Death or life imprisonment and fine.
• Grievous hurt: Up to 7 years imprisonment and fine.
Aspect Key Points
Background Indian Evidence Act, 1872 enacted to consolidate laws relating to evidence.
Electronic Evidence Electronic/digital records have the same legal validity as paper records.
Oral Evidence Permits oral evidence through electronic means, including witness statements
before courts.
Joint Trials Trials involving multiple accused treated as joint trials, even if one accused has
absconded or not responded to an arrest warrant.

Other Initiatives to Counter Risks of Online Gaming
• Section 69A of IT Act, 2000: Empowers Government to block access to illegal websites. E.g., 1,524
betting and gambling websites and mobile apps blocked during 2022-25.
• IT Rules, 2021: Provide norms for online gaming platforms. E.g. Registration of Intermediaries with Self-
regulatory bodies.
• Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023: Section 111 and 112 penalise unlawful economic activities and
cybercrimes and unauthorised betting and gambling respectively.
• Consumer Protection Act, 2019: Prohibits misleading advertisements.
7.4. NATIONAL SPORTS GOVERNANCE ACT, 2025
Why in the news?
Recently, the National Sports Governance Act, 2025 came into force.
Sports governance in India
• Sports regulation falls within jurisdiction of states, as per Entry 33 of State List of Seventh Schedule.
• Existing policies include National Sports Policy (NSP) (1984, revised 2001) and Sports Broadcasting
Signals (Mandatory Sharing with Prasar Bharati) Act, 2007.
o Recently, Cabinet approved NSP 2025, superseding the 2001 policy.
> It is structured around five core pillars, focusing on global excellence, economic and social
development through sports, making sports a people movement, and integrating sports with
education.
Key highlights of Act
Aspect Detail
Establish
National
Sports
Bodies
• Includes National Olympic Committee, National Paralympic Committee, and
National and Regional Sports Federations for each designated sport.
• Affiliated with international bodies and will have affiliated units at state and district
levels.
