7.4.1. NATIONAL SPORTS GOVERNANCE (NATIONAL SPORTS BODIES) RULES, 2026
Why in the news?
Recently, Union Government has notified the National Sports Governance (National Sports Bodies) Rules, 2026
under National Sports Governance Act, 2025.
About National Sports Governance (National Sports Bodies) Rules, 2026
• Each National Sports Body to have a General body and an executive committee.
o Composition of General Body: At least 4 sportspersons of outstanding merit (SOMs), with 50% being
women.
> Eligibility of SOMs: Not less than 25 years of age and must have retired from active sports and
should have not participated in any competitive sport event which leads to selection to represent
a district, State or India for at least one year prior to the date of application.
o Women’s representation in Executive Committee: specific posts to be reserved for women to ensure
at least 4 women members.
• There shall be only one National Olympic Committee and one National Paralympic
Committee, each governing multi-sport disciplines.
National
Sports Board
(NSB)
• Central Government establishes NSB, consisting of a Chairperson and such number
of other Members as may be prescribed.
• Central government will decide its composition and appoint members on
recommendations of search-cum-selection committee.
• Powers and functions
o Grant recognition to any sports organisation as NSB and register their affiliate unit.
o Only recognised bodies will receive funds from central government.
o Issue guidelines for formulation of Code of Ethics by National Sports Bodies.
o Constitute an ad hoc administrative body or to direct the concerned National
Sports Body or their affiliate units,
o Frame Safe Sports Policy for protection and safety of women, minor athletes
and other classes of persons.
• A recognised organisation shall be considered a public authority under RTI Act, 2005.
National
Sports
Tribunal
• To adjudicate “sports-related disputes”.
• Composition: Chairperson and two other members
o Chairperson shall be a person who is or has been, a Judge of Supreme Court or Chief
Justice of a High Court.
o Two members: Secretary to Government of India in Ministry of Law and Justice and
in Department of Sports.
• Vested with power of civil court.
• Exclusion of jurisdiction of Tribunal:
o Any dispute, or conflict arising during Olympic Games, Paralympic Games,
Commonwealth Games, Asian Games.
o Any dispute falling within exclusive jurisdiction of internal disputes resolution
committee of a National Sports Body; or any other tribunal or court; or any
International Federation; or Court of Arbitration for Sports established in Lausanne,
Switzerland.
o Doping related disputes.
Oversight of
elections
• Central government will establish a national panel of electoral officers to oversee
elections of national sports bodies.
• Every national sports body must constitute an electoral panel to oversee elections of
its affiliates.

7.6. PROTECTION OF WOMEN FROM DOMESTIC VIOLENCE ACT
(PWDVA) 2005
Why in the News?
The Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 has completed 20 years.
About PWDV Act, 2005
• Aimed to ensure the fundamental rights of women guaranteed under Articles 14, 15 and 21.
• India, as a signatory to Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women
(CEDAW), was required to legislate specifically against domestic violence, recognizing it as a violation of
human rights.
Key provisions of the Act
Aspect Details
Wide definition of
Domestic
Violence (DV)
• Includes actual abuse or threat of abuse including physical, sexual, emotional,
verbal, and economic abuse.
• Harassment by way of unlawful dowry demands to women victim or her relatives.
Scope Applies to all women in domestic relationships: marriage, live-in relationships,
familial ties, and shared households.
Rights of Women • Right to reside in shared household (irrespective of ownership).
• Provides temporary custody of child.
• Reliefs include protection orders to prevent acts of domestic violence, Monetary
Orders for maintenance for herself and her Children, and Compensation order for
damages caused to her.
Institutional
Framework
• Protection Officers: Appointed to assist victims, coordinate support, provide
legal aid, and ensure enforcement of orders.
o They are required to report the cases of DV to the Magistrate upon receipt of
complaints and to assist Magistrate in discharge of functions.
• Service Providers: NGOs and government agencies to provide counseling,
shelter, medical assistance, and legal help.
• Government Duties: Promote awareness, training, and coordination among
stakeholders.
Procedure and
Enforcement
• Time-bound justice: Woman victim can get immediate civil remedies within 60
days.
• Evidence: Sole testimony of aggrieved woman is admissible as proof, easing the
burden on victim.
• Penalties: Breach of court orders punishable with imprisonment/fine.
7.7. POSH ACT, 2013
Why in the news?
Recently, Supreme Court has expanded the ambit of Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention,
Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 or PoSH, Act 2013.
More on news
• In Dr. Sohail Malik v. Union of India & Anr case 2025, Supreme Court held that a woman harassed at
workplace by a person outside her organisation may file a complaint with Internal Complaints Committee
(ICC) of her own workplace.

• Aimed to ensure the fundamental rights of women guaranteed under Articles 14, 15 and 21.
• India, as a signatory to Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women
(CEDAW), was required to legislate specifically against domestic violence, recognizing it as a violation of
human rights.
Key provisions of the Act
Aspect Details
Wide definition of
Domestic
Violence (DV)
• Includes actual abuse or threat of abuse including physical, sexual, emotional,
verbal, and economic abuse.
• Harassment by way of unlawful dowry demands to women victim or her relatives.
Scope Applies to all women in domestic relationships: marriage, live-in relationships,
familial ties, and shared households.
Rights of Women • Right to reside in shared household (irrespective of ownership).
• Provides temporary custody of child.
• Reliefs include protection orders to prevent acts of domestic violence, Monetary
Orders for maintenance for herself and her Children, and Compensation order for
damages caused to her.
Institutional
Framework
• Protection Officers: Appointed to assist victims, coordinate support, provide
legal aid, and ensure enforcement of orders.
o They are required to report the cases of DV to the Magistrate upon receipt of
complaints and to assist Magistrate in discharge of functions.
• Service Providers: NGOs and government agencies to provide counseling,
shelter, medical assistance, and legal help.
• Government Duties: Promote awareness, training, and coordination among
stakeholders.
Procedure and
Enforcement
• Time-bound justice: Woman victim can get immediate civil remedies within 60
days.
• Evidence: Sole testimony of aggrieved woman is admissible as proof, easing the
burden on victim.
• Penalties: Breach of court orders punishable with imprisonment/fine.
7.7. POSH ACT, 2013
Why in the news?
Recently, Supreme Court has expanded the ambit of Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention,
Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 or PoSH, Act 2013.
More on news
• In Dr. Sohail Malik v. Union of India & Anr case 2025, Supreme Court held that a woman harassed at
workplace by a person outside her organisation may file a complaint with Internal Complaints Committee
(ICC) of her own workplace.

PT 365
 - Polity, Governance and Social Issues
• It enables ICC constituted at aggrieved woman's workplace to exercise jurisdiction over an employee of
a different workplace.
Key provisions of PoSH Act 2013
• Genesis: Vishakha Guidelines laid out by Supreme Court in 1997.
• Definition of Sexual harassment: It includes anyone or more of following unwelcome acts or behaviour
(whether directly or by implication), namely:
o Physical contact or advances;
o A demand or request for sexual favours;
o Making sexually coloured remarks;
o Showing pornography;
o Any other unwelcome physical, verbal, or non-verbal conduct of a sexual nature.
• Definition of workplace: Any place visited by employee arising out of or during course of employment,
including transportation provided by employer for undertaking such a journey.
o A workplace covers both organised and un-organised sectors.
• Aggrieved woman: Covers all women, irrespective of their age or employment status and whether in
organized or unorganized sectors, public or private.
o It also covers clients, customers and domestic workers as well.
• Grievance Redressal Mechanism: Done at two levels:
o Internal Complaint Committee (refer infographics) and Local Complaints Committee (LCC)
> Composition of LCC: Minimum of five members of which half must be women.
✓ Chairperson from eminent women in field of social work and committed to cause of women;
✓ One Member from women working in block, taluka or tehsil or ward or municipality in district;
✓ Two Members (atleast one shall be a woman), from NGOs or associations committed to
cause of women;
✓ Concerned officer (member ex officio) dealing with social welfare or women and child
development in district.
o Tenure of all members of ICC and LCC: 3 Years.
o Both ICC and LCC have powers similar to a civil court.
Other Steps taken to prevent Crime against Women
• National Database on Sexual Offenders (NDSO): Launched by Ministry of Home Affairs to facilitate
investigation and tracking of sexual offenders across country by law enforcement agencies.
• One Stop Centre (OSC) scheme: Provide integrated services like medical aid, police assistance, legal
counselling, temporary shelter etc. to women affected by violence.
• Investigation Tracking System for Sexual Offences: Monitor and track time-bound investigation in
sexual assault cases in accordance with Criminal Law (Amendment) Act 2018.

7.8. FOREIGN CONTRIBUTION (REGULATION) ACT, 2010
Why in the news?
Recently, Supreme Court pulled up the Union Government for non-granting of FCRA renewal for two NGOs due
to violation of Section 7 of FCRA 2010.
About Section 7 of FCRA 2010
• It prohibits any person or organization that has received foreign contribution under the Act from transferring
such funds to any other person or organization.
• Exception: Contributions made by Non-Resident Indian, from his personal savings, through the normal
banking channels, is not treated as foreign contribution.
About Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act (FCRA) 2010
• Foreign contribution means donation, delivery or transfer made by any foreign source of any article,
currency, or security.
o FCRA is implemented by MHA, supported by Intelligence Bureau in approvals and rejections through
investigation on antecedents.
Major Provisions of FCRA Post 2020 amendment
Specifications Detail
Foreign funds • Prior Permission to receive foreign funds though registration with mandatory Aadhaar
submission of every office-bearer of NGOs.
• Prohibits receipt of foreign funds (refer image).
FCRA Account • Designated FCRA Account in such branches of State Bank of India, New Delhi to
receive funds.
o No funds other than foreign contribution can be received or deposited in it and it
can’t be transferred to any other person or NGO.
Validity of
FCRA
registration
• Validity is five years and NGOs are expected to apply for renewal within six months of
date of expiry of registration.
Reduction in
use of foreign
contribution
• Use of funds is limited to purpose for which it was received with a maximum limit of
20% (earlier 50%) to meet administrative expenses.
Submission of
annual
reports
• Compulsory filing of annual returns and in case of contraventions, government can
restrict usage of unutilized foreign contribution after an inquiry.

PT 365
 - Polity, Governance and Social Issues
7.9. WAQF (AMENDMENT) ACT, 2025
Why in the news?
Recently, Parliament passed Waqf (Amendment) Act, 2025.
About Waqf
• Waqf or auqaf (plural of waqf) is a type of Charitable Endowment, where a person donates property for
religious or social welfare such as building mosques, schools, hospitals, etc.
• History:
o Waqf is Arabic and history is linked to the Hadiths.
o In 8th century CE India, Waqf properties were managed by provincial and district officers, like the Sadr-
e Subah and Sadr-e-Sarkar, but central authority had ultimate control.
Key highlights of Waqf (Amendment) Act, 2025
Aspect Key Provisions
Focus Improved management of waqf properties, stakeholder empowerment, and efficient
surveying mechanisms.
Creation of Waqf Only a lawful property owner can create a waqf. Waqf-alal-aulad cannot deny
inheritance rights of heirs, including women.
Mandatory Online
Registration
All waqf properties must be registered on an online portal within 6 months (extension
possible by Waqf Tribunal).
Wrongful
Declaration of
Waqf
Government property cannot be declared waqf.If dispute arises, a State-designated
officer will conduct an inquiry and submit a report to the State Government.
Inspection of
Records
Permitted under Section 75 of Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023.
Acquisition for
Public Purpose
Waqf property may be acquired under RFCTLARR Act, 2013 or other land acquisition
laws, in consultation with the Waqf Board.
Central Waqf
Council
Chaired by Union Minister in charge of Waqf (ex-officio).Empowered to issue
directives to Waqf Boards and exercise suo motu powers.Members include MPs,
Supreme Court/High Court judges, among others.
Related News: UMEED Portal
Union Minority Affairs Minister launched UMEED Central Portal for real-time uploading, verification, and
monitoring of Waqf properties.
About UMEED Portal
• Legislation: Under Unified Waqf Management, Empowerment, Efficiency and Development
(UMEED) Act, 1995.
• Key Features: Creation of a digital inventory with geo-tagging of all Waqf properties, Online grievance
redressal, GIS mapping etc.
• Significance: Enabling greater transparency, accountability, participation of Waqf assets to
healthcare, livelihood generation, social welfare, etc.
Doctrine of Essentiality
Recently, Union Government told the Supreme Court that Waqf is not an essential part of Islam.
About Doctrine of Essentiality
• A jurisprudential principle formulated by Supreme Court (Shirur Mutt Case 1954) to determine which
religious practices qualify as essential and is thereby protected under Article 25 of Constitution.
• Aims to strike a balance between freedom of religion and other fundamental rights.
• Ensures religious practices do not infringe upon the rights to equality, dignity and liberty.

8. GOVERNANCE AND SOCIAL ISSUES
8.1. CENSUS 2027
Why in the News?
Cabinet approves scheme of Conduct of Census of India 2027.
Key Details of Census 2027
• Phased Conduct: The exercise will occur in two phases: (i) Houselisting and Housing Census (April to
September 2026) and (ii) Population Enumeration (PE) (February 2027).
• First Digital Census: Using mobile applications.
• Caste Census Data: Census will capture Caste data during the Population Enumeration phase.
• New Technology: A dedicated Census Management & Monitoring System (CMMS) portal has been
developed for real-time monitoring of the process.
o Census-as-a-service (CaaS) will deliver data to ministries in a clean, machine-readable and actionable
format.
• Participation: The public will be provided with an option to self-enumerate.

PT 365
 - Polity, Governance and Social Issues
8.2. AADHAAR
Why in news?
Union Ministry of Agriculture has made Aadhaar authentication mandatory for individuals and farmer groups
to avail benefits under e-NAM (National Agriculture Market) scheme.
Features of Aadhaar
• Uniqueness: 12-digit unique identification number
and no resident can have a duplicate number since it
is linked to their individual biometrics.
o It is issued by UIDAI and can also be deactivated
or omitted by UIDAI.
• Random Number: It does not capture details like
caste, religion, income, health, geography, etc.
• Proof of address and Identity for residents of India.
Aadhaar is not a proof of citizenship or domicile.
• Scalable technology Architecture: Resident's data is
stored centrally, and authentication can be done
online from anywhere in country.
• Electronics Benefit Transfers: UID-enabled-Bank-
Account network will offer a secure and low-cost
platform to directly remit benefits to residents.
• Targeted Delivery: Central/State Governments to
require Aadhaar for availing benefits/subsidies
under schemes that are funded out of Consolidated
Fund of India or Consolidated Fund of State
(Aadhaar Act, 2016).
Constitutionality of Aadhaar Act (Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v UoI)
• In its 2018 judgment, top court affirmed constitutionality of Aadhaar Act, 2016.
• Key Observations:
o Aadhaar metadata cannot be stored for more than six months.
o Act does not violate fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19 and 21.
o Aadhaar would be mandatory for accessing social welfare schemes, but it cannot be forced on
people for opening bank accounts or for mobile and internet connection.
o State cannot enter into contracts with private corporations for sharing of Aadhaar data, as it
infringes on right to privacy.
Note: Insurance regulator IRDAI has advised insurers not to mandatorily seek Aadhaar and PAN/Form 60 from
customers, existing or new, for KYC purpose. Insurers can accept Aadhaar as one of the documents for KYC,
only when same is offered voluntarily by proposer/policy-holder.
8.3. OVERSEAS CITIZENS OF INDIA (OCI)
Why in the news?
Union Government, under Section 7D of Citizenship Act, 1955, has tightened rules for OCI cardholders facing
serious charges or convictions.
About OCI Cardholders
• Introduced in 2005 through an amendment in Citizenship Act, 1955.
o In 2015, Person of Indian Origin (PIO) card scheme was merged with OCI, deeming all PIO
cardholders as OCI.

PT 365

o Recently, Prime Minister of India announced OCI cards for 6th-generation Indians in Trinidad &
Tobago.
• OCI is not 'dual citizenship'.
• Foreign military personnel, serving or retired, are not entitled for OCI.
• Revocation of OCI (Section 7D of Citizenship Act 1955):
o if an individual is sentenced to at least two years in prison; or
o if a charge sheet is filed for an offense punishable with seven or more years.
• Ground for cancellation of OCI registration:
o Registration obtained by fraud;
o Disaffection towards Constitution of India;
o Interest of sovereignty and integrity of India, security of India, friendly relations of India with any foreign
country, or in interests of general public.
Restrictions related to OCI cardholders
• Does not confer political rights to vote.
• Ineligible for constitutional posts: President (Article 58); Vice-President (Article 66); Judge of Supreme
Court (Article 124) and High Court (Article 217).
• Not eligible for being a member of Lok Sabha/Rajya Sabha/ Legislative Assembly/Legislative Council.
• Not entitled to equality of opportunity in public employment (Article 16).
o Not eligible for Union or State public service, except specific posts designated by Central
Government.
• Special approval required for research, mountaineering, missionary activities, journalism and to visit
Restricted / Protected Areas.
Note: For more details on OCI, PIO and NRI, refer to Appendix at the end of this document.

• Exemption of Passport/Visa for:
o Indian armed forces on duty
o Indian, Nepali, and Bhutanese citizens at specified borders;
o Tibetans with valid registration and special permits;

Key Highlights of Immigration and Foreigners Act, 2025
• Consolidation of Laws: Replaces four statutes- Passport (Entry into India) Act, 1920; Registration of
Foreigners Act, 1939; Foreigners Act, 1946; and Immigration (Carriers’ Liability) Act, 2000.
• Immigration Posts: Central Government empowered to notify designated posts for entry and exit.
• Bureau of Immigration: Established to handle visa issuance and regulate entry, transit, stay, and movement
of foreigners.
• Registration Requirement: Mandatory registration of foreigners with a Registration Officer upon arrival.
• Reporting Obligations: Carriers, educational institutions, and medical institutions must furnish
information relating to foreigners.
• Offences & Penalties: Entry without valid travel documents punishable with up to 5 years’ imprisonment,
₹5 lakh fine, or both.
• Power of Arrest: Police officers of Head Constable rank or above may arrest without warrant.
• Control of Premises: Civil authorities can restrict foreigners’ access to, or order closure of, places
frequented by them.
8.5. LAND REGISTRATION SYSTEM IN INDIA
Why in the News?
Recently, Supreme Court in Samiullah v. State of Bihar case has called for a fundamental reform in country's
land registration and titling system.
More on the News
• Supreme Court of India struck down Bihar's rule making mutation proof mandatory for property
registration, reaffirming that registration records transaction, not ownership.
o It reiterated that document registration under the Registration Act, 1908, only records a transaction,
while mutation (updating revenue records) is a separate process that doesn't confer title.
o Supreme Court in K Gopi v Sub Registrar Case 2025 held Registration Act,1908 does not empower
sub-registrars to check title or ownership with their only mandate being to verify that the paperwork
is in order and the parties have signed it voluntarily.
Land Registration System in India
• Land is a “State subject” and registration of deeds is a concurrent list subject under the Schedule VII of
the Constitution.
o India follows Deeds land registration system (refer infographics).

Measures taken to improve Land Record System in India
• DILRMP: Replace manual presumptive land-title system with a digital conclusive land titling system. It is
a Central Sector Scheme.
• Initiatives under DILRMP
o Unique Land Parcel Identification Number (ULPIN) or Bhu-Aadhar: 14-digit Alpha–numeric unique
ID for each land parcel based on Geo-coordinates to streamline real estate transactions.
o NGDRS or E-Registration: Empowers citizens through online entry of deed, online payment, online
appointment, online admission, document search and certified copy generation.
o Linkage of e-Court with Land Records / Registration Data base: It makes authentic first-hand
information available to Courts resulting in speedy disposal of cases and reduction in land disputes.
o Transliteration of Land Records: To overcome language barrier, transliterate Records of Rights in
local language to any 22 languages listed in Schedule VIII of Constitution.
• Digitalise Land Record System of different States
o Dharani portal/Bhubharati portal Telangana: Modernize land administration by digitizing land
records and streamlining transactions.
o Kaveri portal, Karnataka: Reform land registration.
o Banglarbhumi platform, West Bengal: Provide easy access to land and property records.

the Survey of India.

India and National Informatics
Centre Services Inc (NICSI).

o Continuously Operating
Reference Stations (CORS)
Network: Establishes ground control points for precise geo-referencing and land demarcation.

8.7. TRIBAL GOVERNANCE
Why in the news?
Recently, protests for demand of statehood and
recognition as tribal area under Sixth Schedule
turned violent in UT of Ladakh.
Sixth Schedule
• Enacted under Article 244(2) based on
Bardoloi Committee’s recommendations.
• Empowers Governor to create Autonomous
District Councils (ADCs) & Autonomous
Regional Councils (ARCs) in sixth schedule
area states.
o ADCs: Consist of not more than 30 members (4 nominated by Governor & 26 elected). Presently, there
are 10 ADCs in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.
o ARCs: Covers a specific tribe within a region of autonomous district.
• Power of ADCs and ARCs: Make laws on forest management, agriculture, inheritance, marriage, social
customs etc.
o Collect land revenue, impose taxes, regulate trading, collect royalties from mineral extraction etc.
About Fifth Schedule
• Under PESA, State Government continues to exercise its executive functions within Scheduled Areas.
• Governor has special powers such as modifying laws and establishing Tribes Advisory Council.
• Union Government issued directions to State on Scheduled Areas’ administration but cannot assume to
take over total administration, it would require invocation of Article 356 (President’s Rule).
• Within a State, notification of an area as Scheduled Area takes place through President’s Order.
o Largest administrative unit forming Scheduled Area is District and lowest is cluster of villages in
Block.
o Governor has to submit a report to President regarding administration of such areas, annually or
whenever so required by President.
• Samatha v. State of Andhra Pradesh & Ors, 1997: Supreme Court declared that transfer of tribal land to
private parties for mining was null and void under Fifth Schedule.
Key information about Schedule Tribe (ST)
• Article 342(1): President (by public notification and
after consulting Governor in case of State), specify
which tribes or tribal communities are to be
recognized as STs for that State or UT.
• Article 342(2): Parliament has power to include or
exclude tribes from STs list. This list is State or UT
specific and a community recognized as a ST in one
State need not to be recognized in another State.

PT 365
 - Polity, Governance and Social Issues
Other Tribal Governance Framework
• Ministry of Tribal Affairs (1999): Nodal ministry for integrated socio-economic development of Scheduled
Tribes.
• PESA Act, 1996: Empowers Gram Sabhas in Scheduled Areas.
o Authority over natural resources, land transfers, and local development.
• Customary Tribal Institutions: Adivasi Sabhas/Tribal Panchayats manage culture, resources, and dispute
resolution alongside formal Panchayats.
Major Steps towards Tribal Development
• Pradhan Mantri Janjati Adivasi Nyaya Maha Abhiyan, 2023-24: Focused development of 75 PVTGs across
18 states & 1 UT.
o The mission aims to provide basic facilities such as safe housing, clean drinking water and improved
access to education, health and nutrition, etc in 3 years.
o These objectives are planned to be met through 11 interventions implemented by 9 Ministries.
• Dharti Aaba Janjatiya Gram Utkarsh Abhiyan (DAJGUA): Infrastructure & socio-economic upliftment in
tribal-majority villages with tribal population of 500 or more and at least 50% tribal residents as well as
villages in Aspirational Districts with a tribal population of 50 or more.
• Development Action Plan for Scheduled Tribes: 41 ministries earmark funds annually for tribal
development.
• PM Adi Adarsh Gram Yojana (PMAAGY): Model village development covering ~40% tribal population.
• Entrepreneurship Promotion: Van Dhan Vikas Kendras, PM Janjatiya Vikas Mission, TRIFED/Tribes India
outlets.
8.7.1. ADI KARMAYOGI
Why in the News?
Special Gram Sabhas held across 1 lakh tribal-dominated villages and Tolas adopted the Tribal Village Vision
2030 Declaration as part of Adi Karmayogi Abhiyan.

Key Features of Tribal Village Vision 2030 Declaration
• Village-level planning: Actionable goals in education, health, livelihoods, social & financial inclusion, and
infrastructure.
• Institutional mechanism: Adi Sewa Kendras in every village as single-window service centres.
o Adi Sewa Samay: Villagers contribute 1 hour/week of voluntary service.
About Adi Karmayogi Abhiyan
It is a national movement to build a decentralized
tribal leadership and governance ecosystem.
• Coverage: It marks the world’s largest tribal
grassroots leadership programme, covering
11 crore citizens in 30 States and UTs.
• Key Objectives
o Train and mobilize 20 Lakh tribal change-
makers across 1 Lakh villages in 550
tribal-dominated districts
o Facilitate Village Vision 2030 Planning &
Development Action Plan
o Promote last-mile convergence and
effective service delivery
• It forms a key part of the Janjatiya Gaurav Varsh
(from 15 November 2024 to 15 November 2025)
to Commemorate Bhagwan Birsa Munda's
150th birthday.
• Multi-Department Convergence: Tribal Welfare, Rural Development, Women & Child Development, Jal
Shakti, School Education, and Forest.
8.7.2. FOREST RIGHTS ACT, 2006
Why in the News?
Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006 or Forest Rights
Act (FRA) has completed 20 years in 2026.
About FRA
• Objective: To recognize the rights of forest-dwelling communities who have resided on forest lands for
generations, ensuring sustainable livelihoods and ecological security.
• Nodal Implementation Agency:
Ministry of Tribal Affairs (MoTA) along
with Tribal Departments at State
Level.
• Rights Holders: Forest dwelling
Scheduled Tribes (STs) and Other
Traditional Forest Dwellers (OTFDs).
o OTFD means any member or
community who has for at least
three generations prior to
December 13, 2005 primarily
resided in and who depend on the
forest or forests land for
livelihood needs.

over Vedanta Company’s claim to exploit the hills for bauxite.
8.7.3. PARTICULARLY VULNERABLE TRIBAL GROUPS ( PVTG)
Why in the news?
Recently, Centre has approved 25 new road projects in Tripura (sanctioned under Road Connectivity component
of PM-JANMAN) to improve connectivity in PVTG areas.
About PVTGs
• They are among the most marginalized and vulnerable sections among tribal communities.
• Ministry of Home Affairs has recognized 75 tribal communities as PVTGs based on recommendations of
Dhebar Commission (1960- 61).
• PVTGs are centrally recognized special category from among Scheduled Tribe.
• PVTGs reside in 18 States and UT of A&N Islands. Odisha has highest number of PVTGs.
o A recent centre habitation-level survey estimated the PVTG population to be 45.56 lakh.
o Largest populations: Madhya Pradesh (12.28L), Maharashtra (6.2L), Andhra Pradesh (4.9L).

UNICEF India launched TALASH (Tribal Aptitude, Life Skills and Self-Esteem Hub) Initiative.
About TALASH Initiative
• It is a national- program to support the all-round development (both educational and personal growth) of
students in Eklavya Model Residential Schools (EMRSs).
o EMRS is a central sector scheme under Ministry of Tribal Affairs to provide residential schooling to
tribal children in blocks having more than 50% of ST population.

8.8. JUVENILE JUSTICE SYSTEM IN INDIA
Why in the News?
The recently released report “Juvenile Justice and Children in Conflict with the Law: A Study of Capacity at
the Frontlines” by the India Justice Report.
More on the News
• The report evaluated the functioning of the Indian Juvenile Justice System ten years after enactment of
Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act 2015 (JJ Act, 2015).
• Led by Tata Trusts, in collaboration with civil society organizations, The India Justice Report provides
data-driven insights to track improvements and deficits in the formal justice system.
System of Juvenile Justice in India
• Legal Basis: Governed by the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, which replaced
the JJ Act, 2000; amended by the JJ (Care and Protection of Children) Amendment Act, 2021.
• Objective: Consolidates laws relating to children in conflict with law and children in need of care and
protection, focusing on care, rehabilitation, and social reintegration.
• Definitions:
o Child: Person who has not completed 18 years of age.
o Juvenile: A child below 18 years.
o Child in conflict with law: A child below 18 years alleged or found to have committed an offence.
• Key Provisions of JJ Act, 2015
o Juvenile Justice Boards (JJBs): Established in every district to adjudicate cases of children in conflict
with law.

8.8. JUVENILE JUSTICE SYSTEM IN INDIA
Why in the News?
The recently released report “Juvenile Justice and Children in Conflict with the Law: A Study of Capacity at
the Frontlines” by the India Justice Report.
More on the News
• The report evaluated the functioning of the Indian Juvenile Justice System ten years after enactment of
Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act 2015 (JJ Act, 2015).
• Led by Tata Trusts, in collaboration with civil society organizations, The India Justice Report provides
data-driven insights to track improvements and deficits in the formal justice system.
System of Juvenile Justice in India
• Legal Basis: Governed by the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, which replaced
the JJ Act, 2000; amended by the JJ (Care and Protection of Children) Amendment Act, 2021.
• Objective: Consolidates laws relating to children in conflict with law and children in need of care and
protection, focusing on care, rehabilitation, and social reintegration.
• Definitions:
o Child: Person who has not completed 18 years of age.
o Juvenile: A child below 18 years.
o Child in conflict with law: A child below 18 years alleged or found to have committed an offence.
• Key Provisions of JJ Act, 2015
o Juvenile Justice Boards (JJBs): Established in every district to adjudicate cases of children in conflict
with law.
o Monitoring Mechanism:
National level – National Commission for Protection of Child Rights (NCPCR).
State level – State Commissions for Protection of Child Rights (SCPCR).
o Preliminary Assessment for Heinous Offences:
Applicable when a child aged 16–18 years is alleged to have committed a heinous offence.
JJB assesses mental and physical capacity, ability to understand consequences, and
circumstances of offence.
Children’s Court decides whether the child should be tried as an adult.
o Child Welfare Committees (CWCs):
Constituted in every district by State Governments.

8.8.1. CHILD ADOPTION
Why in the news?
CARA has directed State Adoption Resource Agencies (SARAs) under JJ Act, 2015 (amended 2021) and in line
with Adoption Regulations, 2022 to strengthen counselling support for Child adoption.
Child adoption In India
• Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Women and Child Development
• Primary legislation: Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act of 1956 and Juvenile Justice (Care and
Protection of Children) Act, 2015.
• Nodal Central Agency: Central Adoption Resource Authority (CARA), established under the JJ Act.
• Hague Convention on Protection of Children and Cooperation in Respect of Intercountry Adoption
(1993) ensures ethical, legal, and transparent international adoptions while preventing child trafficking.
• Responsibility of States/UTs: States and UTs implement JJ Act through institutions like SARA, local Child
Welfare Committees, District Child Protection Units (DCPUs).

8.9. ONLINE CONTENT REGULATION
Why in the News?
Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (I&B) has banned public access to several OTT platforms, for
streaming obscene content.
More on the news
• They were banned under Information Technology Act, 2000, and Information Technology (Intermediary
Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021.
• Supreme Court in Ranjit D. Udeshi v. State of Maharashtra (1965) defined obscenity as a reasonable
restriction to free speech (Article 19).
Regulatory framework for regulation of obscene content
• IT Act 2000
o Section 67 and Section 67A: Deal with publication and transmission of obscene content in electronic
form
o Section 69A: Empowers Central Government to direct blocking of public access to information in
specific grounds.
o Section 79: Grants “safe harbour” protection to intermediaries (including OTT platforms) from liability
for third-party content.
> However, they could lose protection if they fail to remove unlawful content upon receiving
notification from government.
• IT rules 2021 under IT Act, 2000 issued by Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (Meity):
Places digital news media and OTT platforms under Ministry of information and broadcasting ministry (MIB).
• Section 294 of Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023: Defines the term obscene, criminalizes the
dissemination of material including those in electronic form.
• Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POSCO) Act: Made sale and distribution of child
pornography illegal.
• Indecent Representation of Women (Prohibition) Act, 1986: Prohibits publication and distribution of
materials containing indecent representations of women.
8.10. GLOBAL GENDER GAP, 2025
Why in the News?
Recently, Global Gender Gap Report 2025 was released by World Economic Forum.
Key Findings of Report
• India:
o Ranked 131st out of 148 countries slipping from 129th position in 2024, although score improved to
0.644 (2025) from 0.641 (2024).
o Bangladesh emerged as best performer in South Asia. Nepal, Sri Lanka, Bhutan ranked better than
India.
• Global:
o Iceland leads the rankings for the 16th year running, followed by Finland, Norway, the United Kingdom
and New Zealand.

Economic
Participation &

8.11. GLOBAL HUNGER INDEX, 2025
Why in the news?
Global Hunger Index (GHI) 2025 has been released by Concern Worldwide and Welthungerhilfe, Non-
Government Organizations from Ireland and Germany respectively.
Key Findings of the report
• India Related Findings:
o India is ranked 102nd among 123 countries assessed with a GHI score of 25.8, which is classified as
serious.
o 1 in 3 Indian children is stunted. Undernourishment affects 172 million people, 13.5 million more than
in 2016.
• Global Findings:
o Achieving Zero Hunger: At current rates, SDG-2 (Zero Hunger by 2030) is out of reach. At least 56
countries are not on track to achieve even low hunger by 2030.

8.12. UNICEF’S CHILD NUTRITION REPORT 2025
Why in News?
Recently, UNICEF has released Child Nutrition Report 2025 titled as ‘Feeding Profit: How Food Environments
are Failing Children’.
Key Findings of UNICEF’s Child Nutrition Report 2025
• First time, Obesity among children and adolescents aged 5–19 (9.4%) surpassed that of underweight
(9.2%).
o Obesity among this age group has tripled since 2000, rising from 3% to 9.4%, while underweight
dropped from nearly 13% to 9.2%.
• Globally, one in twenty children under age of 5 (5%) and one in five children and adolescents aged 5–19
(20%) is living with overweight.
• Low- and middle-income countries are experiencing steepest rise in overweight.
• Obesity rates exceed underweight in every region except sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.
Government Initiatives to improve Nutrition
• POSHAN Abhiyaan (2018): Improve nutritional outcomes for children (0-6 years), adolescent girls, and
mothers.
o Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Women and Child Development.
o Type: Centrally Sponsored.
o POSHAN stands for Prime Minister’s Overarching Scheme for Holistic Nutrition
o Mission POSHAN 2.0 (launched in 2021): It amalgamated various programmes with similar objectives
such as Supplementary Nutrition Programme and POSHAN Abhiyaan under one umbrella.
o Funding pattern:
> For States & UTs with a legislature: 60:40 between Union and State/ UT Government.
> For North-eastern (NER) & Himalayan States: 90:10 between Union and State Government.
> For UTs without a legislature: 100% by Union Government.
• Eat Right India Movement by FSSAI: Ensures safe, healthy, and sustainable food via consumer
awareness, supply-side programs, and school interventions.
• ‘Aaj Se Thoda Kam’ Campaign by FSSAI: Nationwide campaign encouraging gradual reduction of fat,
sugar, and salt intake.
• RUCO (Repurpose Used Cooking Oil) Initiative by FSSAI: Collects used cooking oil to safely repurpose
it for biodiesel or soap, preventing re-entry into the food chain.
Related News: State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World (SOFI) 2025 Report
SOFI is jointly produced with fellow UN agencies IFAD, UNICEF, WFP and WHO.
Key findings of report
• Global hunger decreased to an estimated 8.2% in 2024 from 2022 levels. However, hunger continues to
climb in most subregions of Africa and Western Asia.
• Moderate or severe food insecurity has declined gradually since 2021.
• Food prices rose throughout 2023 and 2024, pushing up the average cost of a healthy diet globally.
• Excluding India, there is an increasing trend in lower-middle-income countries in number of people
unable to afford a healthy diet.

8.13. MATERNITY LEAVE
Why in news?
Recently, Supreme Court in Umadevi v. Government of Tamil Nadu, 2025 ruled that maternity leave is an
essential component of a woman’s reproductive rights, thereby reinforcing constitutional and human rights
of working women.
Key Highlights of Judgment
• Constitutional Protection: A woman’s right to reproductive choices is protected under Article 21, as a part
of personal liberty (Suchita Srivastava v. Chandigarh Administration case).
• Human Rights: Reproductive rights are enshrined in Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) &
include rights to health, privacy, dignity, and equality.
Maternity Benefit Act, 1961
• It provides paid maternity leave to women workers.
• Applicability: Factories, mines, plantations, government establishments, shops and other workplaces
with 10 or more employees. Women covered under Employees’ State Insurance Act, 1948 also receive
maternity benefits.
• Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act, 2017 brought following changes:
o Increased paid maternity leave from 12 weeks to 26 weeks for women with less than two surviving
children.
 Out of these 26 weeks, not more than 8 weeks shall be preceding the expected date of delivery.
o Depending upon the nature of work, Act provides for work from home for nursing mothers.
o Extension of 12 weeks of maternity benefit to women who are expecting after having 2 children,
commissioning mothers and adopting (child below 3 months) mother.
o Applicable to all establishments employing 10 or more women.
o Any establishment which has 50 or more employees shall have crèche facility.
> Section 11A states that employer shall allow 4 visits a day to creche by woman employee.
8.14. ULLAS - NAV BHARAT SAAKSHARTA KARYAKRAM
Why in the News?
Recently, Tripura becomes third state in country, after Mizoram and Goa, to achieve Full Functional Literacy
under ULLAS – Nav Bharat Saaksharta Karyakram (New India Literacy Programme).
More on the News
• Earlier in 2024, Ladakh became the first administrative unit to declare full functional literacy.
o Functional literacy is the ability to read, write, and comprehend information in a way that allows an
individual to function effectively in their daily life.
• According to Ministry of Education, above 95% literacy rate is considered equivalent to Full literacy.
About ULLAS - Nav Bharat Saaksharta Karyakram or New India Literacy Programme (NILP)
Aspect Details
Definition of
Literate
Ability to read, write, perform basic numeracy, and possess digital & financial literacy
(not merely signature ability).
Type Centrally Sponsored Scheme aligned with NEP 2020.
Ministry Ministry of Education
Duration FY 2022–23 to 2026–27
Target Group Adults 15 years and above who missed formal schooling
Target Coverage 5 crore learners (≈ 1 crore per year)
Digital Platform OTLAS (Online Teaching, Learning and Assessment System) developed by NIC

Digital Literacy
Kerala becomes India’s first 100% digitally literate state. The achievement stems from "Digi Keralam"
programme, launched in 2023, with goal of extending digital revolution and e-services to all residents,
irrespective of age.
• It also builds upon Kerala’s earlier Akshaya project, which made Malappuram the first e-literate
district in India.
• Digitally Literate Household means atleast one member (5 years of age or older) can operate computer
and use internet.
Note: Kerala’s declaration of full literacy in 1991 was based on National Literacy Mission (>90% literacy rate).
8.15. SWACHH SURVEKSHAN 2024-25 AWARDS
Why in the news?
Recently, President of India confers Swachh Survekshan 2024-25 awards.
About Swachh Survekshan
• Launched: 2016 under ambit of Swachh Bharat Mission (Urban).
• Institutions involved: Survey is conducted annually by Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA).
o Field Assessment of Data submitted by ULBs are verified by third party agency.
• Objective: To encourage large-scale citizen participation and create awareness about the importance of
making towns and cities a better place to live.
• Components: Swachh Survekshan (Data provided by ULBs, Documents assessment & Field assessment)
and Certification (Based on ODF+/ODF++/Water+ status) with 80% and 20% weightage.
• Swachh Survekshan 2024-25 (theme - Reduce, Reuse, Recycle) marks 9th Annual edition of world's
largest urban cleanliness survey.
Key highlights of Awards (2024-25)
• Five tier population based classification: Million Plus cities (>10lakh), Big cities (3-10 lakh), Medium Cities
(50000-3 lakh), Small Cities (20000-50000), Very Small Cities (<20000).
• Awards: Across following 4 categories:

8.16. KASHI DECLARATION 2025
Why in news?
Kashi Declaration adopted in youth spiritual summit conducted by Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports for drug-
free India.
About Kashi Declaration
• Sets a 5 year roadmap for De-Addiction Movement.
• Affirms a national consensus to treat substance abuse as a multi-faceted public health and societal
challenge.
• Approach to be followed:
o Proposes institutional mechanisms for multi-ministerial coordination, including formation of a Joint
National Committee, annual progress reporting, and national platform for linking affected individuals
to support services.
o Integration of spiritual, cultural, educational, and technological efforts to prevent addiction.
Status of Drug-abuse in India (as per National Survey on Substance Use, 2019)
• Prevalence of Alcohol Use: 14.6% between the age of 10 and 75 years are current users of alcohol.
• Cannabis and Opioids (e.g., heroin) are the next commonly used substances in India.
Initiatives against use of illicit drugs
Global
• Commission on Narcotic Drugs (CND) was established by the UN’s Economic and Social Council
(ECOSOC) resolution in 1946.
• UNODC was established in 1997 to address the interrelated issues of drug control, crime and
international terrorism in all its forms.
• UN Convention against Illicit Traffic in Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances, 1988.
• UN Decade Against Drug Abuse (1991-2000)

8.17. GLOBAL TOBACCO EPIDEMIC, 2025
Why in the News?
The World Health Organization (WHO) released tenth report on the Global Tobacco Epidemic 2025.
More on news
• Maldives became first country to impose generational ban on tobacco.
• Generational Tobacco Ban, or Lifetime Tobacco Ban, refers to a policy that permanently prohibits sale of
cigarettes or other tobacco products to anyone born after a specified date
Key findings of report
• WHO MPOWER measures: Since 2007, 155 countries have implemented at least one of those policy
prescriptions which have benefitted over 6.1 billion people.
o MPOWER Initiative has six components such as Monitor, Protect, Offer help, Warning, Enforce and
Raise taxes.
• Effectiveness of Mass media campaigns: India became first country in 2023, to apply tobacco control
regulations to digital streaming content.
• Taxation: Tobacco tax is least-adopted MPOWER measure. In India, Trend in per capita GDP needed to
buy cigarettes decreased since 2014.
About Tobacco
• Species: Over 60 species, with N. tabacum and N. rustica (Aztec tobacco) commonly cultivated. It
belongs to the nightshade family and indigenous to South America.
• Climate: Frost-free period of 90-120 days from transplanting to final harvest.
• Temperature: Mean daily temperature of 20-30°C with dry harvesting period.
• Soil: Prefers sandy or sandy loam but varies as per variety but sensitive to waterlogging; requires well-
aerated, well-drained soils.
• Rainfall: Minimum 500 mm; not suitable above 1200 mm.
• Production: India is world's 2nd largest producer (after China) and 2nd largest exporter of
unmanufactured tobacco by quantity (after Brazil).
o Major states: Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, Utter Pradesh, Karnataka, West Bengal, Telangana, and
Bihar.
> Gujarat accounts for 45% of production with highest productivity, followed by Andhra Pradesh.
Steps taken to tackle Tobacco epidemic
• Global
o WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC): Adopted in 2003, more than 180
countries have ratified it. (India became a Party to Convention in 2005)
o 3 by 35 Initiative (WHO): To increase prices of any or all of three– tobacco, alcohol, and sugary drinks
by at least 50% by 2035.
• India
o Cigarettes and Other Tobacco Products Act (COTPA), 2003: Prohibits smoking in public places; ban
on sale of tobacco products to minors, etc.
o National Tobacco Control Programme (NTCP).
o Prohibition of Electronic Cigarettes Act, 2019.

8.18. OTHER IMPORTANT NEWS
8.18.1. NEW INITIATIVES FOR GLOBAL AI GOVERNANCE
Recently, UN General Assembly (UNGA) announced to establish two mechanisms within the UN to promote
international cooperation on the governance of Artificial Intelligence (AI).
About the new mechanisms
• Independent International Scientific Panel on Artificial Intelligence: To promote scientific
understanding of AI by issuing evidence-based scientific assessments.
• Global Dialogue on AI Governance: To serve as a platform to discuss international cooperation, share best
practices and lessons learned, and to facilitate open, transparent and inclusive discussions on AI
governance.
Other UN Mechanisms for Global AI Governance
• Pact for the Future: Adopted at the Summit of the Future in September 2024 to chart a path toward
achieving the SDGs and responding to emerging challenges and opportunities.
• Global Digital Compact: Annexed as part of the Pact for Future, it is a comprehensive global framework for
digital cooperation and AI governance.
• AI for Good Global Summit (organized by ITU since 2017): Identified AI applications to advance on the
SDGs and scale such applications for global impacts.
8.18.2. VISAKHAPATNAM DECLARATION
Vishakapatnam Declaration adopted at 28th National Conference on e-Governance, called for a whole-of-
government approach to strengthen civil services with digital competencies, and agile & data-driven
frameworks.
Key Proposals of Vishakapatnam Declaration
• National Vision: Inclusive, citizen-centric, and transparent governance.
• Technology-Driven Governance: Adoption of AI, ML, Blockchain, GIS, IoT, and Data Analytics to deliver
multilingual, real-time, and sector-specific citizen services.
o E.g. Digital India BHASHINI, Digi Yatra, NADRES V2, etc.
• Replication of Successful Models: Focus on nationwide scaling of models like SAMPADA 2.0 (MP), eKhata
(Bengaluru), Rohini Gram Panchayat (Maharashtra), Drone Analytics Monitoring System (DAMS) by
NHAI, etc.
• Grassroots and Inclusive Development:
o Geographical Outreach: To regions with connectivity challenges, like North-East and Ladakh.
• Cybersecurity and Resilience: Focus on Zero-Trust architecture, post-quantum security and AI-
enabled monitoring.
• Agriculture and Sustainability: National Agri Stack for better access to credit, advisories, and markets.
Related News: World’s first AI minister appointed in Albania
Diella, an AI-generated bot will manage and award all public tenders for government projects, aiming to
enhance transparency and minimize corruption.
8.18.3. GLOBAL EDUCATION MONITORING 2024 REPORT
Report is mandated by UNESCO’s ‘Education 2030 Incheon Declaration and Framework for Action’ for
monitoring and reporting on SDG 4 (Quality education) and in the other SDGs.
Key findings
• Disruption of Education Systems: Over past 20 years, schools were closed in at least 75% of extreme
weather events, impacting 5 million people or more.

8.18.4. PERFORMANCE GRADE INDEX (PGI) 2.0
Recently, Ministry of Education released ‘Performance Grade Index (PGI) 2.0’ for 2022-23 & 2023-24.
About PGI
• Genesis: PGI was launched in 2017 and later revamped as PGI 2.0 in 2021.
• Released by: Ministry of Education
• Data sources: Unified District Information System for Education Plus (UDISE+), National Achievement
Survey (NAS), PM-POSHAN portal, PRABAND portal and Vidyanjali Portal.
• Purpose: PGI holistically assesses performance of school education systems across States and UTs.
• Structure: PGI structure comprises of total weightage of 1000 points across 73 indicators, which are
grouped under 2 Categories viz., Outcome and Governance & Management.
o These categories are further divided into 6 domains, viz., Learning Outcomes (LO), Access (A),
Infrastructure & Facilities (IF), Equity (E), Governance Processes (GP) and Teacher Education & Training
(TET).
• Evaluation: Across 10 Grades out of 1000 points.
Key Findings of Report
• No state/UT achieved the top four grades (Daksh, Utkarsh, Atti-Uttam, Uttam).
• Top Performer: Only Chandigarh attained Grade Prachesta-1.
• Lowest Performer: Meghalaya is the only state in the tenth grade (Akanshi-3).
• Inter-State Disparity: The report highlights that this disparity has reduced.
• Overall Trends:
o 24 states/UTs improved scores in 2023-24 compared to 2022-23.
o 12 states/UTs including Bihar, Andaman and Nicobar Islands, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Karnataka,
etc. saw score declines.
8.18.5. UDISE+ 2024-25 REPORT
Unified District Information System for Education Plus (UDISE+) 2024-25 Report was recently released by
Ministry of Education.
• It aligns its findings as per NEP 2020 recommendations and has shifted to individual student-wise data
collection mechanism.
Major Findings of UDISE+ 2024-25 Report
• Total number of teachers crossed 1 crore mark in 2024–25 (rise of 6% from 2022-23) for the first time
since beginning of UDISE+.
• Pupil-Teacher Ratios (PTRs) have surpassed NEP's recommended 1:30 ratio.
o Present PTR: Foundational (10), Preparatory (13), Middle (17), and Secondary levels (21).
• Drop-out rates: Decreased to 2.3% (Preparatory), Middle (3.5) and Secondary (8.2).
• Gross Enrolment Ratio: Improved to 90.3% (Middle) and 68.5% (Secondary) levels
• Zero enrolment (decreased by 6%) and single-teacher schools (38% decrease) shown progressive
changes.

8.18.6. MULTIDISCIPLINARY EDUCATION AND RESEARCH IMPROVEMENT IN
TECHNICAL EDUCATION (MERITE) SCHEME
Union Cabinet approved Budgetary Support for MERITE Scheme.
About MERITE Scheme
• Type: Central Sector Scheme
• Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Education
• Objective: To improve the quality, equity and governance in technical education covering all States/UTs.
o Aligned with National Education Policy 2020.
• Budget: Rs.4200 crore for a period from 2025-26 to 2029-30.
o Includes external assistance of Rs.2100 crore from World Bank as loan.
• Beneficiaries: 275 technical institutions comprising 175 engineering institutions and 100 polytechnics.
8.18.7. GLOBAL NETWORK OF AGE-FRIENDLY CITIES AND COMMUNITIES
(GNAFCC)
Kozhikode city has been honoured with a membership in the WHO’s GNAFCC.
About GNAFCC
• Established: 2010
• Members: 1300 members from 51 countries.
• Aim: To connect cities, communities and organizations worldwide with the common vision of making their
communities great places to grow older in.
• Objectives:
o Inspire change by showing what can be done and how.
o Connect cities and communities worldwide to facilitate the exchange of information, knowledge and
experience.
o Support cities and communities to find appropriate innovative and evidence-based solutions.
8.18.8. UNIVERSAL IMMUNIZATION PROGRAMME (UIP)
Recently, WHO/UNICEF 2024 provides the world’s largest and most comprehensive dataset on immunization
trends for vaccinations against 14 diseases.
Key Findings
• Global: In 2024, 89% of infants globally received at least one dose of the diphtheria, tetanus and pertussis
(DTP) containing vaccine.
• India: Reduced its number of zero-dose children by 43% in 2024 (from 1.6 million in 2023 to 0.9 million in
2024).

8.18.6. MULTIDISCIPLINARY EDUCATION AND RESEARCH IMPROVEMENT IN
TECHNICAL EDUCATION (MERITE) SCHEME
Union Cabinet approved Budgetary Support for MERITE Scheme.
About MERITE Scheme
• Type: Central Sector Scheme
• Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Education
• Objective: To improve the quality, equity and governance in technical education covering all States/UTs.
o Aligned with National Education Policy 2020.
• Budget: Rs.4200 crore for a period from 2025-26 to 2029-30.

8.18.7. GLOBAL NETWORK OF AGE-FRIENDLY CITIES AND COMMUNITIES
(GNAFCC)
Kozhikode city has been honoured with a membership in the WHO’s GNAFCC.
About GNAFCC
• Established: 2010
• Members: 1300 members from 51 countries.
• Aim: To connect cities, communities and organizations worldwide with the common vision of making their
communities great places to grow older in.
• Objectives:
o Inspire change by showing what can be done and how.
o Connect cities and communities worldwide to facilitate the exchange of information, knowledge and
experience.
o Support cities and communities to find appropriate innovative and evidence-based solutions.

o Nationwide (9): Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Polio, Measles, Rubella, Childhood Tuberculosis,
Hepatitis B and Meningitis & Pneumonia.
o Region-specific (3): Rotavirus diarrhoea, Pneumococcal Pneumonia, and Japanese Encephalitis.
• Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Health & Family Welfare
• UIP become a part of Child Survival and Safe Motherhood Programme in 1992.
o Since, 1997, immunization activities have been an important component of National Reproductive &
Child Health Programme and are currently one of key areas under National Rural Health Mission
since 2005.
• A child is considered fully immunized after receiving all vaccinations as per National schedule within first
year of life.
• Achievements: Polio-Free India (2014), Neonatal tetanus elimination (2015).
• Key Initiatives: Intensified Mission Indradhanush 5.0 (IMI 5.0) campaign with special focus on improvement
of Measles and Rubella vaccination coverage, U-WIN Portal etc.
8.18.9. STRAY DOG MANAGEMENT
Recently, Supreme Court has modified its suo motu order, directing that stray dogs in Delhi-NCR be released to
their original areas after deworming and vaccination, instead of being confined to shelters.
Provisions related to Stray Dogs in India
• Constitution: Article 243W mandates municipalities to control stray dog population.
o Article 51A(g): Fundamental Duty “to have compassion for living creatures”.
• Animal Birth Control Rules, 2023: under Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960 seek to control stray
dog through sterilisation and vaccination.
• Court Judgements:
o Jallikattu Case 2014: Interpreted right to life and liberty (Article 21) as extending to animals.
o People for Elimination of Stray Trouble v. Animal Welfare Board of India: Suspended the killing of all
street dogs, including troublemakers.

Charter Act, 1853 Ended East India Company’s patronage in recruitment, enjoining recruitment to
be through an open competition.
Lord Macaulay
Committee, 1854
Recommended a merit-based competitive examination system.
First Indian to succeed
in ICS
Satyendranath Tagore in 1864
Aitchinson
Commission, 1887
Recommended reorganization of services into three groups i.e. Imperial,
Provincial and Subordinate.
Government of India
Act, 1919
First provided for creation of Public Service Commission and recommended that
one-third recruitments to be held in India. From 1922 onwards, exams began to
be held in India also (First in Allahabad).
Lee Commission, 1924 Following its recommendations, first Public Service Commission was
established in, 1926, under GoI Act 1919. It was headed by Sir Ross Barker.
Government of India
Act, 1935
Envisaged a Public Service Commission for Federation and a Provincial Public
Service Commission. When act came into effect in 1937, Public Service
Commission became Federal Public Service Commission (FPSC).
Constitutional mandate Upon India’s independence in 1947, FPSC was headed by its first Indian
chairman, H.K. Kripalani. With the adoption of Constitution on 26th January
1950, FPSC assumed its present constitutional status (Article 315) as UPSC.

9.5. APPENDIX 5: KEY SPORTS REGULATORY BODIES
Body Key Details
Ministry of
Youth and
Sports (MYAS)
• Functioned under overall guidance of Minister of State (Independent Charge) for Youth
Affairs & Sports.
• In 2008, two separate Departments, namely, Department of Youth Affairs and
Department of Sports, were created under Ministry.
o Each department under the charge of a Secretary to Government of India.
• Responsible for formulating national sports policy, funding and implementing
development programmes.
Sports
Authority of
India (SAI)
• Set up in 1984 as a Society registered of Societies Act, 1860 to carry forward the legacy
of 9th Asian Games held in New Delhi in 1982 under Department of Sports.
• Fully funded by Government of India and is under Administrative control of MYAS.
• Lays down policies and guidelines for sports delivery systems.
Indian Olympic
Association
(IOA)
• Established in 1927 with Sir Dorabji Tata and Dr. A.G. Noehren as Founding President
and Secretary General respectively.
• Governed by a 32-member Executive Council. Election for Executive Council is held
once in every 4 years.
• Recognised by MYAS and registered as a Non-Profit Organisation under Societies
Registration Act, 1860.
• Governing body for Olympic Movement and Commonwealth Games in India.
• Affiliated member of International Olympic Committee (IOC), Commonwealth Games
Federation (CGF), Olympic Council of Asia (OCA) and Association of National Olympic
Committees (ANOC).
• Responsible for India’s participation in Olympic Games and other international multi-
sports events.

9.6. APPENDIX 6: KEY ACHIEVEMENTS OF NATIONAL EDUCATIONAL
POLICY 2020
Particulars Details
School education
(Economic Survey 2024-
25)
• Near universal GER at primary level: 93 %
• Decline in School dropout rates: 1.9 % for primary, 5.2 % for upper primary, and
14.1 % for secondary levels.
• Digitalization: Increase in schools equipped with computers (38.5% to 57.2%)
and internet facilities (22.3% to 53.9%) from 2023-2024 to 2019-20.
Higher Education (18–23
age group) (Economic
Survey 2024-25)
• Increase in GER: From 23.7% (2014-15) to 28.4% (2021-22)
• Increase in Total Higher Education Institutions (HEIs): 13.8% from 2014- 15 to
2022-23.
Rural schools (Annual
Status of Education
Report (ASER) 2024)
• FLN Directives: Over 80% of 15,728 rural schools reported receiving FLN
directives.
• Overall school enrolment rates among 6-14 age group: > 95% for close to 20
years.
• Drop in Proportion of 15-16-year-old children not enrolled in school: From
13.1% in 2018 to 7.9% in 2024.
Teacher Training • 12.97 lakh teachers trained under NISHTHA (Teacher Training Programme).
Inclusivity • 7.58 lakh girls enrolled in inclusive residential schools.
Internationalisation • Prestigious institutions like Deakin and Wollongong Universities (Australia) and
University of Southampton (UK) have campuses in India
Literacy • Ladakh became the first fully literate administrative unit followed by Mizoram,
Goa and Tripura
Multilingualism • National Tests like CUET, JEE (Mains), and NEET (UG) are conducted in 12 Indian

9.6. APPENDIX 6: KEY ACHIEVEMENTS OF NATIONAL EDUCATIONAL
POLICY 2020
Particulars Details
School education
(Economic Survey 202425)
• Near universal GER at primary level: 93 %
• Decline in School dropout rates: 1.9 % for primary, 5.2 % for upper primary, and
14.1 % for secondary levels.
• Digitalization: Increase in schools equipped with computers (38.5% to 57.2%)
and internet facilities (22.3% to 53.9%) from 2023-2024 to 2019-20.
Higher Education (18–23
age group) (Economic
Survey 2024-25)
• Increase in GER: From 23.7% (2014-15) to 28.4% (2021-22)
• Increase in Total Higher Education Institutions (HEIs): 13.8% from 2014- 15 to
2022-23.
Rural schools (Annual
Status of Education
Report (ASER) 2024)
• FLN Directives: Over 80% of 15,728 rural schools reported receiving FLN
directives.
• Overall school enrolment rates among 6-14 age group: > 95% for close to 20
years.
• Drop in Proportion of 15-16-year-old children not enrolled in school: From
13.1% in 2018 to 7.9% in 2024.
Teacher Training • 12.97 lakh teachers trained under NISHTHA (Teacher Training Programme).
Inclusivity • 7.58 lakh girls enrolled in inclusive residential schools.
Internationalisation • Prestigious institutions like Deakin and Wollongong Universities (Australia) and
University of Southampton (UK) have campuses in India
Literacy • Ladakh became the first fully literate administrative unit followed by Mizoram,
Goa and Tripura
Multilingualism • National Tests like CUET, JEE (Mains), and NEET (UG) are conducted in 12 Indian
languages
